payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]

  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=South Twenty Four Parganas.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 44
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Sales Supervisor, Retail Trade
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: South Twenty Four Parganas
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 44-year-old female from rural South 24 Parganas, West Bengal, is currently married and speaks Bengali as her first language. She works as a sales supervisor in retail trade with below-primary education, and her lifestyle includes a preference for home-cooked meals and participation in Bengali festivals. Her daily routine involves managing store operations and maintaining a calm, methodical approach to work.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.travel_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, prefers short, budget‑friendly trips to nearby gems like Kaziranga National Park and the tea estates of Jorhat, enjoys weekend hill retreats in Shillong, and they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi to experience deeper spiritual rhythms.
payload.culinary_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, favors tangy masor tenga fish curry, earthy bamboo‑shoot tarkari, and sweet rice pitha, enjoys nibbling on roasted peanuts and fresh aloo pitika, and while they rarely cook elaborate meals, they skillfully prepare these home‑style dishes using garden‑grown vegetables and local spices.
payload.persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, a quiet market hawker who blends meticulous inventory tricks with heartfelt community care, they hum folk tunes while balancing a handwritten ledger and dreaming of a women‑led cooperative shop.
payload.cultural_background: Shilpi Rajbanshi is a Bodo woman from the rural district of Baksa in Assam. Her cultural roots blend the traditional customs of the Bodo tribe and the Rajbanshi community, known for their agrarian heritage, folk music, and dance such as Bagurumba. Living in the Bodoland Territorial Region, she participates in Bodo festivals like Bisu and celebrates mainstream Hindu festivals like Diwali and Durga Puja, reflecting a syncretic religious practice. As a street vendor, her daily routine is intertwined with the local market culture, where she trades in fresh produce, homemade snacks, and small handicrafts, reinforcing community bonds and mutual support.
payload.linguistic_background: Shilpi’s mother tongue is Bodo, which she speaks fluently at home and in the market. She learned Assamese in school and uses it comfortably for everyday communication and trade negotiations. Her formal education up to middle school gave her basic literacy in Hindi and English, allowing her to read simple signs, write price tags, and handle occasional interactions with tourists. She can read and write Bodo (written in Devanagari adapted script) and Assamese scripts, and she has conversational proficiency in Hindi and rudimentary English.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Through years of operating a roadside stall, Shilpi has built a practical skill set that mirrors her curiosity‑driven yet methodical nature. She is adept at sourcing fresh vegetables, fruits, and homemade snacks, applying a systematic inventory system that ensures minimal waste. Her pricing strategy blends market research with traditional bargaining, reflecting both innovative thinking and respect for established norms. She handles cash transactions and basic bookkeeping with reliability, and she communicates politely yet assertively in Bodo, Assamese, and Hindi to negotiate with customers. When faced with supply challenges, she employs ‘jugaad’ – creative improvisation – to maintain stock. Her compassionate disposition leads her to assist elderly shoppers and fellow vendors, fostering a cooperative market environment.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Street vending", "Product sourcing", "Competitive pricing", "Inventory management", "Cash handling", "Basic bookkeeping", "Customer negotiation", "Multilingual communication (Bodo, Assamese, Hindi)", "Jugaad problem solving", "Compassionate assistance to vulnerable customers"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In her quiet moments, Shilpi enjoys reading Bodo folktales and listening to traditional Bagurumba music. She practices weaving on a small loom, creating simple handicrafts for personal use. Her garden at home allows her to grow seasonal vegetables, which she tends with care. She also likes gentle walks in the nearby hills and practices meditation to maintain inner calm. Occasionally she volunteers at community health camps, offering assistance to the elderly.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Bodo folktales", "Listening to Bagurumba music", "Weaving on a small loom", "Home gardening", "Walking in nearby hills", "Meditation", "Volunteering at community health camps"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shilpi aims to grow her modest hawking stall into a small cooperative shop that can provide a stable income for her family and serve the local community. She aspires to learn basic business management and accounting, possibly through adult education programs, to handle larger inventory and diversify into selling local crafts and tea. Over the next five years, she hopes to mentor other women vendors, create a supportive network, and eventually use her earnings to secure her children's education and improve household infrastructure.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 30-year-old female hawker from Baksa district, Assam, with middle education, currently married, and fluent in Bodo and Assamese. She works in a rural market, managing inventory and trade while engaging in community-oriented activities. Her lifestyle includes early-morning walks and yoga, with a preference for local cultural practices.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Baksa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 30
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Hawker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Baksa
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 30-year-old female hawker from Baksa district, Assam, with middle education, currently married, and fluent in Bodo and Assamese. She works in a rural market, managing inventory and trade while engaging in community-oriented activities. Her lifestyle includes early-morning walks and yoga, with a preference for local cultural practices.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Baksa District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|BDH-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Boro]] | DOB [[DOB|1994-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|30]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Hawker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0156]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaya Boro]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-0042]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Baksa]]
Chief complaint: Patient presents with persistent anxiety, sleep disturbances, and mood swings for past 3 months.

History of Present Illness:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Boro]] is a 30-year-old female hawker from Baksa district who reports increased anxiety levels, difficulty sleeping (2-3 hours per night), and frequent mood fluctuations. Symptoms began after increased work stress and financial concerns. Patient describes racing thoughts, palpitations, and difficulty concentrating at work. No suicidal ideation reported.

Examination:
- Mental status: Alert and oriented, cooperative
- Mood: Anxious, labile
- Speech: Normal rate and rhythm
- Thought process: Logical, no delusions or hallucinations
- Insight: Fair

Assessment:
1. Generalized Anxiety Disorder, moderate
2. Insomnia, secondary to anxiety
3. Adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood

Treatment Plan:
1. Prescribed Sertraline 50mg daily, to be taken in morning
2. Referral to counseling services at District Mental Health Program
3. Sleep hygiene education provided
4. Follow-up in 2 weeks
5. Advised to reduce caffeine intake and maintain regular sleep schedule

Prescription:
Sertraline 50mg - 1 tablet daily
Take with food in morning
Refill after 1 month

Patient counseled on medication adherence and importance of follow-up. Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaya Boro]] present during consultation and educated about supportive care measures.

payload.professional_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar, a 27‑year‑old agro‑entrepreneur from the fertile floodplains, blends curiosity‑driven experimentation with meticulous record‑keeping to improve paddy and mustard yields, leverages mobile market‑price apps for better price discovery, and mentors fellow women farmers through the local cooperative.
payload.linguistic_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar speaks Assamese fluently and writes it confidently, uses functional English for market negotiations and radio listening, and understands Hindi well enough for official communications, reflecting her organized approach to language learning.
payload.sports_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar follows cricket passionately, cheering for the regional Ranji team and the Indian national side, participates in weekly village kabaddi matches, and practices sunrise yoga to stay flexible and manage stress, though she sometimes skips sessions after a long day in the fields.
payload.arts_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar enjoys listening to folk Bihu songs, especially those sung by Zubeen Garg, watches Bollywood films starring Ranbir Kapoor, partakes in traditional embroidery, and occasionally knits scarves during monsoon evenings, reflecting her blend of modern curiosity and respect for heritage.
payload.travel_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar prefers short weekend trips to nearby tea garden estates and riverbank picnics, dreams of trekking in Darjeeling and exploring the Sundarbans, plans trips meticulously using budget apps, yet often postpones travel during peak farming seasons to meet family responsibilities.
payload.culinary_persona: Anuprabha Majumdar favors tangy masor tenga made with mustard oil, steaming pitha stuffed with coconut, and seasonal mustard greens, enjoys picking fresh bamboo shoots for home cooking, has moderate cooking skills, and often chooses a simple boiled fish over restaurant meals when time is tight.
payload.persona: Anuprabha Majumdar is a methodical agro‑entrepreneur who merges traditional farming with digital tools, worries about market swings, and unwinds by dancing Bihu after a long day.
payload.cultural_background: Anuprabha Majumdar hails from Nagaon, a fertile district in Assam’s heartland. She belongs to a traditional Assamese farming family where rice paddies and mustard fields have shaped daily life for generations. The rural setting weaves together the Assamese language, Bihu celebrations, folk music, and a deep connection to the Brahmaputra floodplains. While rooted in agrarian customs, she also engages with the broader Indian cultural tapestry through regional festivals, market exchanges, and inter‑community ties.
payload.linguistic_background: She grew up speaking Asamiya (Assamese) as her first language and can read and write the Assamese script with confidence. Through her middle‑school education she acquired functional English, sufficient for basic correspondence, market negotiations and radio listening. Hindi, learned as a second language, enables her to understand official communications and interact with traders from other states. Her literacy is solid in Assamese and Hindi, while her English proficiency remains conversational, reflecting her organized and methodical approach to learning.
payload.skills_and_expertise: She is proficient in crop planning for paddy, mustard, and seasonal vegetables, applying both ancestral wisdom and simple modern techniques. Her expertise includes preparing organic fertilizers from farm waste, managing pests using neem‑based solutions, and providing basic livestock care for cows and goats. She maintains farm accounts, tracks expenses, and negotiates market prices with traders, while also coordinating with the local farmers’ cooperative for seed supply and collective marketing. Her methodical nature ensures accurate record‑keeping and timely execution of farm tasks.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Crop planning", "Organic fertiliser preparation", "Pest management (Neem-based)", "Livestock care", "Farm accounting", "Market negotiation", "Cooperative coordination"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She enjoys participating in Bihu dance during the spring and autumn festivals, cooking traditional Assamese dishes such as masor tenga and pitha, listening to folk music, and reading devotional poetry. Gardening, especially growing medicinal herbs, and occasional knitting are also part of her routine. In her leisure time, she watches regional TV serials and shares tea with neighbours, balancing social interaction with moments of quiet reflection.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Bihu dance", "Cooking Assamese cuisine", "Listening to folk music", "Reading devotional poetry", "Herb gardening", "Knitting", "Watching TV serials", "Tea with neighbours"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Anuprabha’s short‑term goal is to boost the yield and profitability of her family’s paddy and mustard fields by adopting sustainable, organic practices and using mobile market‑price apps for better price discovery. In the medium term, she aims to establish a small agro‑processing unit that produces mustard oil and pickles, creating an additional income stream for her household and providing training opportunities for other women in the village. Ultimately, she aspires to become a respected member of the local farmers’ cooperative, influencing policies that support small‑holder resilience and promoting community‑wide adoption of climate‑smart agriculture.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female cultivator from Nagaon, Assam, with middle education, is currently married and speaks Assamese as her first language. She works in agriculture and resides in a rural zone, where she manages farming activities and is concerned about market price fluctuations. payload.system_prompt:

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Nagaon.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Assamese.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Cultivator, General
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Nagaon
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female cultivator from Nagaon, Assam, with middle education, is currently married and speaks Assamese as her first language. She works in agriculture and resides in a rural zone, where she manages farming activities and is concerned about market price fluctuations. Her lifestyle includes using mobile apps for market information and engaging in traditional cultural practices like dancing Bihu.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
অপাৰেচন নোট — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Nagaon Civil Hospital]] ভৰ্তি [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] ৱাৰ্ড [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Jalali Begum]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] শল্য চিকিৎসক [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
পদ্ধতি / ফলাফল: ৰোগীজনৰ ৬ মাহ ধৰি দীৰ্ঘদিনীয়া কাহ আৰু ওজন হ্ৰাস হোৱা দেখা দিছিল। চিটি স্কেনত সোঁফালৰ ওপৰৰ খণ্ডত গহ্বৰযুক্ত ঘা আৰু প্লিউৰাৰ ডাঠ হোৱা দেখা গৈছিল। টিবি-প্ৰভাৱিত হাওঁফাওঁৰ কলাৰ ডিকৰ্টিকেচন আৰু ৱেজ ৰিচেকচনৰ সৈতে সোঁফালৰ থোৰাকোটমী কৰা হৈছিল। অপাৰেচনৰ সময়ত পোৱা ফলাফলত ব্যাপক প্লিউৰেল আসঞ্জন আৰু কেচিয়েটিং গ্ৰেনুলোমা দেখা গৈছিল। অপাৰেচনৰ পিছৰ অৱস্থা কোনো জটিলতা নোহোৱাকৈ আছিল। ৰোগীক DOTS থেৰাপী অব্যাহত ৰাখিবলৈ পৰামৰ্শ দিয়া হৈছে।
[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Abdul Kalam]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-2024-0815-001]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Nagaon Civil Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Jalali Begum]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with chronic cough and weight loss for 6 months. CT scan revealed right upper lobe cavitary lesion with pleural thickening. Right thoracotomy performed with decortication and wedge resection of TB-affected lung tissue. Intraoperative findings showed extensive pleural adhesions and caseating granulomas. Post-op course uneventful. Patient advised DOTS therapy continuation.

[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Abdul Kalam]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-2024-0815-001]]

payload.professional_persona: Hanik Haq is a seasoned mica screener at a rural mining operation, applying razor-sharp visual judgment and hands-on troubleshooting to separate premium sheets from low-grade ore while informally supervising crews with a blend of curiosity-driven improvisation and practical grit, and they thrive on spontaneous problem-solving.
payload.linguistic_persona: Hanik Haq is fluent in Bengali and conversational in Assamese, possesses functional Hindi and modest English that suffice for basic paperwork and daily interactions, and they switch languages effortlessly according to the person they are speaking with.
payload.sports_persona: Hanik Haq is an avid participant in village cricket matches, representing the local Karimganj Cricket Club and cheering for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL, while also enjoying occasional evening walks along the pond to stay fit, and they relish the camaraderie of team celebrations.
payload.arts_persona: Hanik Haq is a devoted listener of Bengali Baul legends like Paban Das Baul and Assamese folk maestros such as Zubeen Garg, while also being a regular viewer of Bollywood classics featuring Shah Rukh Khan, and they frequently quote lines from 'Dilwale Dulhania Le Jayenge' during gatherings.
payload.travel_persona: Hanik Haq prefers short road trips to nearby tea garden towns like Jorhat, weekend getaways to Shillong for cooler weather, occasional pilgrimages to local shrines, while dreaming of an affordable train journey to Delhi for a Diwali reunion with extended family, and they balance travel plans with modest budgeting.
payload.culinary_persona: Hanik Haq enjoys hearty Bengali biryani served during Eid, relishes Assamese fish curry (machher jhol) with mustard greens and seasonal kalijira pitha, delights in sweet mishti doi, but usually prefers buying these dishes from trusted local vendors rather than cooking them personally, and they occasionally help their wife stir the pot on festive days.
payload.persona: Hanik Haq blends a tinkerer’s curiosity with a community-first mindset, juggling mica grading, spontaneous gadget-making, weekend cricket, and occasional forgetfulness about appointments, all while they keep a steady eye on family stability.
payload.cultural_background: Hanik Haq hails from the Bengali-Muslim community of Karimganj, a border town in rural Assam. His upbringing blends the cultural practices of Bengali families, such as celebrating Eid with traditional biryani and listening to Baul songs, with the Assamese rural lifestyle of agrarian festivals, community gatherings, and a strong sense of neighbourhood solidarity. The region’s multicultural tapestry, shaped by proximity to Bangladesh and a history of trade in mica, informs his respect for diverse customs while staying rooted in modest, family-centred values.
payload.linguistic_background: Hanik is fully fluent in Bengali, the language of his household, and can read and write the Bangla script with ease. His daily work and social interactions in Karimganj have made him equally comfortable conversing in Assamese, and he can understand and write basic Assamese as well. Hindi, taught in school, is his third language, enabling him to communicate with migrants and in official settings, though his reading and writing are functional rather than advanced. With a middle school education, his English skills are modest—he can understand simple instructions and fill out occupational forms, but rarely engages in extended conversation in English.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Hanik’s core expertise lies in mica screening, where he accurately distinguishes high-grade mica sheets from low-grade material through keen visual inspection and tactile assessment. He is proficient in operating hand-powered and simple motorised sorting tables, maintaining safety standards, and recording quality metrics for downstream processing. His on-the-job experience has honed strong hand-eye coordination, quick decision-making, and the ability to adapt to variations in ore composition—a reflection of his practical curiosity. Additionally, he possesses good teamwork and supervisory skills, often coordinating groups of labourers during peak extraction periods, and he can communicate job instructions in Bengali, Assamese and Hindi. He is also adept at basic equipment maintenance and troubleshooting, using makeshift ‘jugaad’ solutions when resources are limited.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Mica grading and visual inspection", "Operating hand and motorised sorting tables", "Safety compliance in mining", "Hand-eye coordination", "Quick decision making", "Team coordination and supervisory", "Multilingual communication (Bengali, Assamese, Hindi)", "Basic equipment maintenance and troubleshooting", "Improvisational problem-solving (jugaad)"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Hanik enjoys social gatherings, especially village cricket matches and community feasts where he can share stories and jokes. He is an avid listener of Bengali Baul songs and Assamese folk music, often humming them while working. In his spare time, he likes to fish at the nearby pond, tend a small vegetable garden, and experiment with simple home-made gadgets—a reflection of his curious streak. He also follows Bollywood movies and cricket broadcasts on TV, and he participates in religious and charitable activities during Ramadan and Eid. Reading the Qur’an and local folklore provides him with both spiritual grounding and entertainment.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Village cricket matches", "Listening to Bengali Baul and Assamese folk music", "Fishing at local ponds", "Vegetable gardening", "Home-made gadget tinkering (jugaad)", "Watching Bollywood movies and cricket on TV", "Participating in Ramadan and Eid community activities", "Reading the Qur’an and local folklore"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hanik aims to move from a floor screener to a supervisory role overseeing quality control in the mica mine, seeking on-the-job training and short courses in mineral grading and occupational safety. He also dreams of setting up a modest family-run mica processing unit that adds value to the raw ore, leveraging his practical know-how and community connections. Improving his family's financial stability, supporting his children's higher education, and gaining recognition as a reliable leader in his workplace motivate him. While he prefers flexible, hands-on work over rigid office settings, he is open to learning basic management techniques and computer-based record-keeping.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Hanik Haq, 51, is a married screener with middle education working in rural Assam. He resides in Karimganj district and is fluent in Bengali and Assamese. His lifestyle includes weekend cricket and a preference for local Bengali cuisine.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Karimganj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 51
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Screener, Mica
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Karimganj
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Hanik Haq, 51, is a married screener with middle education working in rural Assam. He resides in Karimganj district and is fluent in Bengali and Assamese. His lifestyle includes weekend cricket and a preference for local Bengali cuisine.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, a street vendor in the Bodoland market, combines systematic inventory management with creative ‘jugaad’ pricing, and they deliver reliable service while quietly optimizing stock to minimise waste.
payload.linguistic_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, fluent in their mother tongue and adept at Assamese, they use conversational Hindi and basic English to negotiate at the market, and can read and write both Bodo and Assamese scripts for signboards and ledgers.
payload.sports_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, enjoys early‑morning hill walks and regular yoga sessions, follows the Assam cricket team and NorthEast United FC, and they occasionally play badminton at the local women’s club.
payload.arts_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, finds solace in listening to Bagurumba folk tunes and Zubeen Garg’s Assamese hits, weaves simple looms for decor, and they regularly read Bodo folktale collections while also appreciating Bollywood performances by Ranveer Singh.
payload.travel_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, prefers short, budget‑friendly trips to nearby gems like Kaziranga National Park and the tea estates of Jorhat, enjoys weekend hill retreats in Shillong, and they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi to experience deeper spiritual rhythms.
payload.culinary_persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, favors tangy masor tenga fish curry, earthy bamboo‑shoot tarkari, and sweet rice pitha, enjoys nibbling on roasted peanuts and fresh aloo pitika, and while they rarely cook elaborate meals, they skillfully prepare these home‑style dishes using garden‑grown vegetables and local spices.
payload.persona: Shilpi Rajbanshi, a quiet market hawker who blends meticulous inventory tricks with heartfelt community care, they hum folk tunes while balancing a handwritten ledger and dreaming of a women‑led cooperative shop.
payload.cultural_background: Shilpi Rajbanshi is a Bodo woman from the rural district of Baksa in Assam. Her cultural roots blend the traditional customs of the Bodo tribe and the Rajbanshi community, known for their agrarian heritage, folk music, and dance such as Bagurumba. Living in the Bodoland Territorial Region, she participates in Bodo festivals like Bisu and celebrates mainstream Hindu festivals like Diwali and Durga Puja, reflecting a syncretic religious practice. As a street vendor, her daily routine is intertwined with the local market culture, where she trades in fresh produce, homemade snacks, and small handicrafts, reinforcing community bonds and mutual support.
payload.linguistic_background: Shilpi’s mother tongue is Bodo, which she speaks fluently at home and in the market. She learned Assamese in school and uses it comfortably for everyday communication and trade negotiations. Her formal education up to middle school gave her basic literacy in Hindi and English, allowing her to read simple signs, write price tags, and handle occasional interactions with tourists. She can read and write Bodo (written in Devanagari adapted script) and Assamese scripts, and she has conversational proficiency in Hindi and rudimentary English.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Through years of operating a roadside stall, Shilpi has built a practical skill set that mirrors her curiosity‑driven yet methodical nature. She is adept at sourcing fresh vegetables, fruits, and homemade snacks, applying a systematic inventory system that ensures minimal waste. Her pricing strategy blends market research with traditional bargaining, reflecting both innovative thinking and respect for established norms. She handles cash transactions and basic bookkeeping with reliability, and she communicates politely yet assertively in Bodo, Assamese, and Hindi to negotiate with customers. When faced with supply challenges, she employs ‘jugaad’ – creative improvisation – to maintain stock. Her compassionate disposition leads her to assist elderly shoppers and fellow vendors, fostering a cooperative market environment.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Street vending", "Product sourcing", "Competitive pricing", "Inventory management", "Cash handling", "Basic bookkeeping", "Customer negotiation", "Multilingual communication (Bodo, Assamese, Hindi)", "Jugaad problem solving", "Compassionate assistance to vulnerable customers"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In her quiet moments, Shilpi enjoys reading Bodo folktales and listening to traditional Bagurumba music. She practices weaving on a small loom, creating simple handicrafts for personal use. Her garden at home allows her to grow seasonal vegetables, which she tends with care. She also likes gentle walks in the nearby hills and practices meditation to maintain inner calm. Occasionally she volunteers at community health camps, offering assistance to the elderly.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Reading Bodo folktales", "Listening to Bagurumba music", "Weaving on a small loom", "Home gardening", "Walking in nearby hills", "Meditation", "Volunteering at community health camps"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shilpi aims to grow her modest hawking stall into a small cooperative shop that can provide a stable income for her family and serve the local community. She aspires to learn basic business management and accounting, possibly through adult education programs, to handle larger inventory and diversify into selling local crafts and tea. Over the next five years, she hopes to mentor other women vendors, create a supportive network, and eventually use her earnings to secure her children's education and improve household infrastructure.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 30-year-old female hawker from Baksa district, Assam, with middle education, currently married, and fluent in Bodo and Assamese. She works as a street vendor, managing inventory and pricing while engaging in community care. Her lifestyle includes early-morning walks and yoga, with a preference for local cultural activities.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Baksa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 30
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Hawker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Baksa
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 30-year-old female hawker from Baksa district, Assam, with middle education, currently married, and fluent in Bodo and Assamese. Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]

- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]

- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.user_prompt:

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=South Twenty Four Parganas.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 44
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Sales Supervisor, Retail Trade
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: South Twenty Four Parganas
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 44-year-old female from rural South 24 Parganas, West Bengal, currently married, works as a sales supervisor in retail trade with below-primary education. Her first language is Bengali, and she follows a routine of early morning walks and weekly yoga. Clinically, she presents with a calm demeanor, occasional forgetfulness of alarms, and a habit of hoarding sweets.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: An organized elder named Rajni Das coordinates community festivals, manages household budgeting with meticulous care, and they mentor younger women in traditional weaving and herbal remedies.
payload.linguistic_persona: An oral multilingual named Rajni Das speaks Bodo fluently, understands Assamese and Bengali, and they rely on family members for any written communication.
payload.sports_persona: An enthusiastic community member named Rajni Das enjoys playing Ludo with grandchildren, takes daily walks along the Brahmaputra riverbank, and they cheer for the Assam cricket team and NorthEast United FC during televised matches.
payload.arts_persona: An expressive elder named Rajni Das sings traditional folk lullabies, listens to classic Bollywood films starring Amitabh Bachchan, and they relish the soulful music of Bhupen Hazarika during quiet evenings.
payload.travel_persona: An adventure‑in‑spirit elder named Rajni Das travels by bus to the nearby Tezpur market, visits Guwahati for temple festivals, and they dream of a pilgrimage to the Sikkim monasteries one day.
payload.culinary_persona: An accomplished cook named Rajni Das prepares onla, mitha madua, and sukhmi using mustard oil, bamboo shoots, and fresh herbs, and they delight guests with these dishes during Bwisagu and family gatherings.
payload.persona: Rajni Das blends routine devotion, community stewardship, and cultural storytelling, and they juggle modest finances, health quirks, and a love for classic Bollywood cinema.
payload.cultural_background: Rajni Das is a member of the Bodo community, a recognised Scheduled Tribe in Assam. She lives in the rural hinterland of Sonitpur, where agrarian life and the Brahmaputra floodplains shape daily routines. Her cultural world blends Bodo tribal customs—such as the Bwisagu New Year celebration, traditional weaving (gamosa), folk music, and reverence for nature spirits—with mainstream Assamese Hindu practices, including temple worship and festivals like Durga Puja. The close‑knit village setting fosters strong communal bonds, inter‑generational respect, and adherence to age‑old rituals that honour the cycles of the seasons and agricultural harvests.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajni’s first language is Bodo, which she speaks fluently but cannot read or write due to her lack of formal schooling. She learned Assamese as a second language to communicate with neighbours, buy groceries, and attend the local market; she understands spoken Assamese well and can follow basic oral instructions, though she is not literate in its script. Her third language, Bengali, is acquired through listening to Bengali families nearby and occasional television programmes; she can comprehend simple conversations. All three languages are used primarily in oral contexts, and she relies on family members or neighbours for any written communication such as notices, medication labels or bank slips.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajni is skilled in managing a large household with limited resources, a talent honed over decades of homemaking. She excels at traditional Bodo cooking, preparing dishes such as onla, mitha madua, and sukhmi. Her weaving abilities allow her to produce bamboo mats and gamosa cloths for everyday use and festive occasions. She coordinates community events, especially festivals like Bwisagu and Durga Puja, ensuring smooth logistics and hospitality. Rajni possesses practical knowledge of herbal remedies and natural medicine, often preparing simple treatments for common ailments. She handles household budgeting with careful jugaad, stretching the family’s modest income to meet all needs. Additionally, she provides reliable childcare and eldercare, and is an engaging oral storyteller, preserving local folktales for younger generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: [ "Household management", "Traditional Bodo cooking", "Weaving (bamboo mats & gamosa)", "Community event coordination", "Herbal remedy knowledge", "Budgeting with jugaad", "Childcare and eldercare", "Oral storytelling", "Hospitality and hosting" ]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rajni finds joy in singing traditional Bodo folk songs and lullabies, weaving bamboo mats and gamosa cloths, and tending a small kitchen garden of vegetables and herbs. She loves preparing festive dishes for occasions like Bwisagu and Durga Puja, and looks forward to community melas where she can chat with neighbours. Storytelling is a favorite pastime; she often narrates local legends to her grandchildren. Rajni also participates actively in family puja and village religious ceremonies. In quieter moments, she enjoys listening to radio programs, playing simple board games like Ludo with the kids, and watching classic Bollywood movies.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: [ "Singing Bodo folk songs", "Weaving bamboo mats & gamosa", "Gardening (vegetables & herbs)", "Cooking festive dishes", "Attending village melas", "Storytelling", "Participating in puja ceremonies", "Playing board games (Ludo) with grandchildren", "Watching classic Bollywood movies", "Listening to radio programs" ]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajni’s main ambition is to nurture the younger generation by passing on Bodo cultural knowledge, traditional recipes, and ethical values. She hopes to see her grandchildren complete higher education and secure stable livelihoods. Though she has no formal career aspirations, she wishes to remain active in the village by helping organize festivals, mentoring other women in household management and traditional crafts, and offering guidance in family matters. Maintaining her health so she can support her husband and continue her role as a respected elder in the community is also a priority.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
