GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Madhya Pradesh, district=Mandsaur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Madhya Pradesh
- district: Mandsaur
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Shiv Lal, a 42-year-old male teacher in Mandsaur, Madhya Pradesh, is currently married with a graduate education and works in pre-primary education. He is an urban resident who speaks Hindi as his first language and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes daily puja, yoga, and tending to a mango tree, reflecting a disciplined yet flexible routine.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
MUST tag [[APPOINTMENT_ID|…]] whenever an appointment is mentioned.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text: [[PATIENT_NAME|Shiv Lal]]: आपका सर्जरी अपॉइंटमेंट [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] पर [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Mandsaur]] 21-मई 10:30 डॉ. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]] के साथ। MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]। कृपया [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] पर पुष्टि करें।
payload.generated_text_en: [[PATIENT_NAME|Shiv Lal]]: Your surgery appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Mandsaur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]. Please confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].

payload.professional_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a seasoned farmer who blends deep knowledge of millet, pulses and animal husbandry with market negotiation skills, and they are actively pursuing solar‑drip irrigation and a small diesel tractor to modernise their farm while championing a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag.
payload.linguistic_persona: Pandurang Godekar speaks fluent Kannada, picks up functional Hindi and basic English through years of market chatter, yet they remain illiterate, relying on oral tradition, folk sayings, and radio programmes to navigate daily transactions and agricultural advice.
payload.sports_persona: Pandurang Godekar follows Karnataka’s cricket team with fervor, attends local Kabaddi matches during village fairs, and they maintain personal fitness through early‑morning cattle walks and occasional sessions of traditional wrestling (kusti) with fellow farmers.
payload.arts_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a devoted fan of Karnataka folk singer K. V. Gopal and bhajan maestro Shivraj Bhat, they regularly tune into Yakshagana recordings featuring legend Dr. Karanth, and often hum these melodies while tending their fields.
payload.travel_persona: Pandurang Godekar prefers short, purpose‑driven trips to nearby towns like Hubli and Bengaluru for market dealings and their sons’ school visits, yet they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a leisurely stay at Mysore Palace during the Dasara festival when finances permit.
payload.culinary_persona: Pandurang Godekar favors hearty jowar rotti served with spicy sambar made from toor dal, drumsticks, and tamarind, they occasionally prepare coconut‑based chutney with coriander, and delight in the occasional lemon‑rice (pulao) with peanuts during village fairs.
payload.persona: Pandurang Godekar, a 49‑year‑old farmer who blends curiosity with practical ‘jugaad’, they worry about market swings yet tirelessly work to modernise their fields while keeping village traditions alive.
payload.cultural_background: Pandurang comes from a traditional agrarian community in Gadag, a historic town in northern Karnataka. His family follows Kannada customs, celebrating Ugadi, Sankranti, and local temple festivals with folk dances like Yakshagana and devotional music. Being a Hindu in a rural setting, he participates in community rituals at the village shrine and observes seasonal harvest ceremonies. The regional culture also values practical ingenuity - the age-old 'jugaad' mindset that influences his farming practices.
payload.linguistic_background: Pandurang's mother tongue is Kannada, which he speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with his family and neighbours. He cannot read or write in any language, but his decades of market interaction have given him functional spoken English and Hindi, enabling him to negotiate prices, understand basic instructions, and follow radio programmes about agriculture. He relies on oral tradition and memory, often using folk sayings to convey complex ideas. His linguistic repertoire reflects a pragmatic blend of regional, national, and market languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Pandurang possesses deep practical knowledge of rain-fed and irrigated crop production, especially millet, pulses, and oilseeds native to Karnataka. He is adept at animal husbandry, managing cattle and goats for milk and draught power. Over the years he has honed market-oriented skills such as price negotiation, product grading, and leveraging local traders to sell produce. He can maintain basic farm machinery, repair bullock-drawn ploughs, and apply low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops. His expertise also includes seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts and maintaining seed banks of traditional varieties.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Crop cultivation (millet, pulses, oilseeds)', 'Animal husbandry (cattle, goats)', 'Market negotiation and pricing', 'Product grading and quality control', 'Basic farm machinery maintenance', 'Low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops', 'Seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts', 'Seed preservation of traditional varieties', 'Community coordination for festivals and fairs', 'Oral communication and memory retention']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Pandurang enjoys listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans on the radio, especially those dedicated to Lord Shiva. He spends his free time preparing traditional dishes like jowar rotti and sambar for family gatherings. He walks his cattle in the fields at dawn, which he finds therapeutic. He is fond of attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali, where he watches folk performances and participates in games. In quieter moments, he likes reciting simple mantras and reminiscing about his youth through storytelling.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans', 'Preparing traditional dishes (jowar roti, sambar)', 'Morning walks with cattle', 'Attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali', 'Reciting simple mantras and storytelling', 'Watching folk performances', 'Playing community games', 'Talking with fellow farmers about market trends']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Pandurang aims to modernise his farm while preserving his ancestral knowledge. He dreams of acquiring a small diesel tractor and solar-powered drip irrigation to increase yields and reduce water usage. He wants to establish a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag to collectively market produce, negotiate better prices, and access credit facilities. Ensuring that his two sons receive formal education is a personal priority, so he hopes to provide them with enough income to send them to school. Ultimately, he aspires to become a respected elder in his village, guiding younger farmers on sustainable practices and safeguarding the local grain varieties.
payload.occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 49-year-old male farmer from Gadag, Karnataka, currently married, with a market-oriented crop and animal production occupation and illiterate education. He speaks Kannada and resides in a rural zone, where he manages agricultural operations while navigating market fluctuations. His lifestyle includes daily cattle walks and traditional wrestling, with limited formal education but functional communication skills.

payload.professional_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a seasoned farmer who blends deep knowledge of millet, pulses and animal husbandry with market negotiation skills, and they are actively pursuing solar‑drip irrigation and a small diesel tractor to modernise their farm while championing a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag.

payload.sports_persona: Pandurang Godekar follows Karnataka’s cricket team with fervor, attends local Kabaddi matches during village fairs, and they maintain personal fitness through early‑morning cattle walks and occasional sessions of traditional wrestling (kusti) with fellow farmers.
payload.arts_persona: Pandurang Godekar is a devoted fan of Karnataka folk singer K. V. Gopal and bhajan maestro Shivraj Bhat, they regularly tune into Yakshagana recordings featuring legend Dr. Karanth, and often hum these melodies while tending their fields.
payload.travel_persona: Pandurang Godekar prefers short, purpose‑driven trips to nearby towns like Hubli and Bengaluru for market dealings and their sons’ school visits, yet they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a leisurely stay at Mysore Palace during the Dasara festival when finances permit.
payload.culinary_persona: Pandurang Godekar favors hearty jowar rotti served with spicy sambar made from toor dal, drumsticks, and tamarind, they occasionally prepare coconut‑based chutney with coriander, and delight in the occasional lemon‑rice (pulao) with peanuts during village fairs.
payload.persona: Pandurang Godekar, a 49‑year‑old farmer who blends curiosity with practical ‘jugaad’, they worry about market swings yet tirelessly work to modernise their fields while keeping village traditions alive.
payload.cultural_background: Pandurang comes from a traditional agrarian community in Gadag, a historic town in northern Karnataka. His family follows Kannada customs, celebrating Ugadi, Sankranti, and local temple festivals with folk dances like Yakshagana and devotional music. Being a Hindu in a rural setting, he participates in community rituals at the village shrine and observes seasonal harvest ceremonies. The regional culture also values practical ingenuity - the age-old 'jugaad' mindset that influences his farming practices.
payload.linguistic_background: Pandurang's mother tongue is Kannada, which he speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with his family and neighbours. He cannot read or write in any language, but his decades of market interaction have given him functional spoken English and Hindi, enabling him to negotiate prices, understand basic instructions, and follow radio programmes about agriculture. He relies on oral tradition and memory, often using folk sayings to convey complex ideas. His linguistic repertoire reflects a pragmatic blend of regional, national, and market languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Pandurang possesses deep practical knowledge of rain-fed and irrigated crop production, especially millet, pulses, and oilseeds native to Karnataka. He is adept at animal husbandry, managing cattle and goats for milk and draught power. Over the years he has honed market-oriented skills such as price negotiation, product grading, and leveraging local traders to sell produce. He can maintain basic farm machinery, repair bullock-drawn ploughs, and apply low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops. His expertise also includes seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts and maintaining seed banks of traditional varieties.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Crop cultivation (millet, pulses, oilseeds)', 'Animal husbandry (cattle, goats)', 'Market negotiation and pricing', 'Product grading and quality control', 'Basic farm machinery maintenance', 'Low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops', 'Seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts', 'Seed preservation of traditional varieties', 'Community coordination for festivals and fairs', 'Oral communication and memory retention']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Pandurang enjoys listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans on the radio, especially those dedicated to Lord Shiva. He spends his free time preparing traditional dishes like jowar rotti and sambar for family gatherings. He walks his cattle in the fields at dawn, which he finds therapeutic. He is fond of attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali, where he watches folk performances and participates in games. In quieter moments, he likes reciting simple mantras and reminiscing about his youth through storytelling.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Karnataka folk songs and bhajans', 'Preparing traditional dishes (jowar roti, sambar)', 'Morning walks with cattle', 'Attending village fairs during Sankranti and Diwali', 'Reciting simple mantras and storytelling', 'Watching folk performances', 'Playing community games', 'Talking with fellow farmers about market trends']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Pandurang aims to modernise his farm while preserving his ancestral knowledge. He dreams of acquiring a small diesel tractor and solar-powered drip irrigation to increase yields and reduce water usage. He wants to establish a farmer’s cooperative in Gadag to collectively market produce, negotiate better prices, and access credit facilities. Ensuring that his two sons receive formal education is a personal priority, so he hopes to provide them with enough income to send them to school. Ultimately, he aspires to become a respected elder in his village, guiding younger farmers on sustainable practices and safeguarding the local grain varieties.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Shankarappa]] [[AGE|49]] [[GENDER|Male]] ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸಕ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Hegde]]
ಪ್ರಕ್ರಿಯೆಯ ದಿನಾಂಕ: 15 ಮೇ 2024.
ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಗೆ ಮುನ್ನಿನ ರೋಗನಿರ್ಣಯ: T10-T11 ಕಶೇರುಖಂಡದ ಎಪಿಡ್ಯೂರಲ್ ಬಾವು ಮತ್ತು ಬೆನ್ನುಹುರಿಯ ಸಂಕೋಚನದೊಂದಿಗೆ ಕ್ಷಯರೋಗದ ಬೆನ್ನುಮೂಳೆಯ ಉರಿಯೂತ (ಪಾಟ್ಸ್ ಕಾಯಿಲೆ).
ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರದ ರೋಗನಿರ್ಣಯ: T10-T11 ಕಶೇರುಖಂಡದ ಎಪಿಡ್ಯೂರಲ್ ಬಾವು ಮತ್ತು ಬೆನ್ನುಹುರಿಯ ಸಂಕೋಚನದೊಂದಿಗೆ ಕ್ಷಯರೋಗದ ಬೆನ್ನುಮೂಳೆಯ ಉರಿಯೂತ (ಪಾಟ್ಸ್ ಕಾಯಿಲೆ).
ಕಂಡುಬಂದ ಅಂಶಗಳು: ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ಸಮಯದಲ್ಲಿ ಕಂಡುಬಂದ ಅಂಶಗಳು T10 ಮತ್ತು T11 ಕಶೇರುಖಂಡದ ಕಾಯಗಳನ್ನು ಒಳಗೊಂಡಿರುವ ದೊಡ್ಡದಾದ, ಶೀತವಾದ ಬಾವು ಮತ್ತು ಹಿಂಭಾಗದ ಅಂಶಗಳ ಗಮನಾರ್ಹ ನಾಶವನ್ನು ಬಹಿರಂಗಪಡಿಸಿದವು. ಥೆಕಲ್ ಸ್ಯಾಕ್ ಮತ್ತು ಬೆನ್ನುಹುರಿಯ ಮೇಲೆ ಎಪಿಡ್ಯೂರಲ್ ಸಂಗ್ರಹಣೆಯಿಂದ ತೀವ್ರವಾದ ಸಂಕೋಚನವಿತ್ತು. ಡಿಬ್ರೈಡ್‌ಮೆಂಟ್ ನಂತರ, ಬೆನ್ನುಮೂಳೆಯ ಕಾಲುವೆಯನ್ನು ಸಮರ್ಪಕವಾಗಿ ಡಿಕಂಪ್ರೆಸ್ ಮಾಡಲಾಯಿತು.
ಪ್ರಕ್ರಿಯೆ: ಹಿಂಭಾಗದ ಮಧ್ಯರೇಖೆಯ ವಿಧಾನವನ್ನು ತೆಗೆದುಕೊಳ್ಳಲಾಯಿತು. T9, T10, T11 ಮತ್ತು L1 ರಲ್ಲಿ ಪೆಡಿಕಲ್ ಸ್ಕ್ರೂಗಳನ್ನು ಇರಿಸಲಾಯಿತು. ಸ್ಥಿರತೆಯನ್ನು ಒದಗಿಸಲು ಒಂದು ರಾಡ್ ಅನ್ನು ಬಾಗಿಸಿ ಸಂಪರ್ಕಿಸಲಾಯಿತು. ಹಿಂಭಾಗದ ಅಂಶಗಳನ್ನು ಡಿಕಾರ್ಟಿಕೇಟ್ ಮಾಡಲಾಯಿತು ಮತ್ತು ಮೂಳೆ ಗ್ರಾಫ್ಟ್ ಅನ್ನು ಅನ್ವಯಿಸಲಾಯಿತು. ಗಾಯವನ್ನು ತೊಳೆದು ಪದರಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಮುಚ್ಚಲಾಯಿತು.
ರೋಗಿಯು ಪ್ರಕ್ರಿಯೆಯನ್ನು ಚೆನ್ನಾಗಿ ಸಹಿಸಿಕೊಂಡರು. ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರ ನರಮಂಡಲದ ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಯು ಕೆಳ ತುದಿಗಳ ಸ್ನಾಯು ಶಕ್ತಿಯಲ್ಲಿ ಸುಧಾರಣೆಯನ್ನು ತೋರಿಸಿದೆ.
ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರದ ಯೋಜನೆ: ಕ್ಷಯರೋಗ ವಿರೋಧಿ ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯನ್ನು ಮುಂದುವರಿಸಿ. 24 ಗಂಟೆಗಳ ಕಾಲ ಐಸಿಯು ಮೇಲ್ವಿಚಾರಣೆ. ಅಗತ್ಯವಿದ್ದಂತೆ ನೋವು ನಿವಾರಕ. ದೈಹಿಕ ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆ ಸಮಾಲೋಚನೆ.
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri]] ಅವರಿಗೆ ಪ್ರಕ್ರಿಯೆ ಮತ್ತು ರೋಗಿಯ ಸ್ಥಿರ ಸ್ಥಿತಿಯ ಬಗ್ಗೆ ಮಾಹಿತಿ ನೀಡಲಾಯಿತು.
[[BED_NUMBER|OT-1]]
[[MRN|MRN-GAD-2024-49001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-GAD-2024-49001]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Gadag Institute of Medical Sciences]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-GAD-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Shankarappa]] [[AGE|49]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Hegde]]
Date of procedure: 15th May 2024.
Pre-operative diagnosis: Tuberculous spondylitis (Pott's disease) of T10-T11 vertebra with epidural abscess and spinal cord compression.
Post-operative diagnosis: Tuberculous spondylitis (Pott's disease) of T10-T11 vertebra with epidural abscess and spinal cord compression.
Findings: Intra-operative findings revealed a large, cold abscess involving the T10 and T11 vertebral bodies with significant posterior element destruction. There was a marked compression of the thecal sac and spinal cord by the epidural collection. After debridement, the spinal canal was adequately decompressed.
Procedure: A posterior midline approach was taken. Pedicle screws were placed at T9, T10, T11, and L1. A rod was contoured and connected to provide stability. The posterior elements were decorticated and bone graft was applied. The wound was irrigated and closed in layers.
The patient tolerated the procedure well. Neurological examination post-operatively showed improvement in motor power in the lower limbs.
Post-operative plan: Continue anti-tubercular therapy. ICU monitoring for 24 hours. Analgesia as required. Physiotherapy consultation.
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri]] was informed about the procedure and the patient's stable condition.
[[BED_NUMBER|OT-1]]
[[MRN|MRN-GAD-2024-49001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-GAD-2024-49001]]

payload.sports_persona: Nirmala Hatti enjoys playing volleyball during village gatherings, follows the Royal Challengers Bangalore cricket team with fervor, participates in weekly jogging sessions along the sugarcane fields, and occasionally joins a local women's kabaddi squad, reflecting their energetic, sociable nature and love for team camaraderie.
payload.arts_persona: Nirmala Hatti passionately performs Kolata folk dance, sings Kannada bhavageete, and accompanies community events on the tambura, while also cherishing the poetry of Kuvempu and the classic songs of Dr. Rajkumar, often leading group performances that showcase their creative spirit and collaborative mindset.
payload.travel_persona: Nirmala Hatti has explored Mysore during the Dasara celebrations, attended a CNC training workshop in Bangalore, and dreams of trekking the Western Ghats in Kerala and visiting Delhi's historic monuments, preferring budget bus travel and meticulously planning itineraries around family commitments.
payload.culinary_persona: Nirmala Hatti enjoys cooking akki rotti and ragi mudde with fresh coconut chutney, relishes the tangy sambar and crunchy banana chips, prefers traditional South Indian spices like mustard seeds and curry leaves, and while not a frequent experimental cook, they love sharing these dishes with friends during festivals, embodying hospitality and a simple palate.
payload.persona: Nirmala Hatti is a diligent young wood‑working operator who blends village hospitality with a relentless curiosity for CNC technology, often humming folk tunes while tightening bolts.
payload.cultural_background: Nirmala belongs to the Kannada‑speaking agrarian community of Mandya district in Karnataka. Growing up in a rural setting, she has been immersed in the region's rich traditions of sugarcane farming, folk music, and the community‑oriented values of the Vokkaliga culture. Her family follows the customs of South Indian Hindu households, celebrating festivals like Ugadi, Sankranti, and Deepavali with elaborate feasts and traditional rituals. The close‑knit village environment has fostered her sense of cooperation, hospitality, and respect for elders.
payload.linguistic_background: Nirmala's mother tongue is Kannada, which she reads, writes, and speaks fluently. She received middle school education in a government school where Kannada was the primary medium of instruction. She has functional proficiency in Hindi, able to understand basic instructions and communicate with neighboring villagers, though she is not comfortable reading Hindi scripts. Her English is limited to workplace terminology related to wood‑working machines and simple everyday conversation.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Nirmala is skilled in operating various wood‑working machines, such as band saws, planers, and CNC routers, in a small‑scale workshop. She can perform routine maintenance, troubleshoot mechanical issues, and ensure safety protocols are followed. Her hands‑on experience includes basic carpentry, assembling furniture components, and material handling. She is adept at reading simple technical drawings, using measuring tools, and coordinating with team members to meet production targets.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Woodworking machine operation', 'Basic carpentry', 'Machine maintenance', 'Technical drawing interpretation', 'Measurement and tool handling', 'Team coordination', 'Safety compliance', 'Material handling']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In her free time, Nirmala enjoys participating in local folk dances like Kolata, singing traditional Kannada bhavageete, and playing the tambura during community gatherings. She likes reading simple Kannada novels and storybooks, gardening in her family’s small sugarcane field, and cooking traditional dishes such as akki rotti and ragi mudde for friends and relatives. She also volunteers in village clean‑up drives and helps organize cultural events during festivals.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Folk dancing (Kolata)', 'Singing Kannada bhavageete', 'Playing tambura', 'Reading Kannada literature', 'Gardening', 'Cooking traditional Karnataka dishes', 'Community volunteering', 'Organizing cultural events']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Nirmala aspires to become a senior machine operator or workshop supervisor, mastering advanced CNC technology. She aims to acquire formal technical training in woodworking engineering, enabling her to improve productivity and quality in the local agro‑based industry. In the long term, she wishes to set up her own small furniture workshop that provides stable livelihoods for her family and creates job opportunities for other village youth, while maintaining environmentally friendly practices.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Mandya.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Wood Working Machine Operatives, Other
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Mandya
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female wood-working machine operator from rural Karnataka, Mandya district, with middle education and Kannada as her primary language. She is never married and works in a small workshop, demonstrating strong safety habits and a curiosity for advanced technology. Clinically, she is likely exposed to occupational hazards like noise and dust, and may have limited access to specialized healthcare.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ಟಿಪ್ಪಣಿ — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mandya District Hospital]] ದಾಖಲೆ [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] ವಾರ್ಡ್ [[WARD_NUMBER|General-A]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi Kumar]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]] ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸಕ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Hegde]]
ಕಾರ್ಯವಿಧಾನ / ಸಂಶೋಧನೆಗಳು: ರೋಗಿಯು 22 ವರ್ಷದ ಮಹಿಳೆ, ಮರಗೆಲಸದ ಯಂತ್ರದ ಚಾಲಕಿ. ಅವರು ಮೂರು ದಿನಗಳ ಹಿಂದೆ ಕೆಲಸದ ಸ್ಥಳದಲ್ಲಿ ಎಡಗೈಗೆ ಆಘಾತಕಾರಿ ಗಾಯಕ್ಕೆ ಒಳಗಾಗಿದ್ದಾರೆ. ಅವರು ಟೇಬಲ್ ಗರಗಸವನ್ನು ಬಳಸುವಾಗ ಆಳವಾದ ಗಾಯವಾಗಿದೆ ಎಂದು ವರದಿ ಮಾಡಿದ್ದಾರೆ. ಪರೀಕ್ಷೆಯ ಸಮಯದಲ್ಲಿ, ಹೆಬ್ಬೆರಳಿನ ಸ್ನಾಯುವಿನ ಮೇಲೆ 4 ಸೆಂ.ಮೀ. ಉದ್ದದ ಗಾಯವಿದ್ದು, ಅದರ ಕೆಳಗಿರುವ ಸ್ನಾಯುರಜ್ಜು ಕಾಣಿಸುತ್ತಿದೆ. ಫ್ಲೆಕ್ಸರ್ ಪಾಲಿಸಿಸ್ ಲಾಂಗಸ್ ಸ್ನಾಯುರಜ್ಜು ಭಾಗಶಃ ಕತ್ತರಿಸಲ್ಪಟ್ಟಿದೆ. ಸೋಂಕಿನ ಯಾವುದೇ ಲಕ್ಷಣಗಳಿಲ್ಲ. ಕೈಯ ಎಕ್ಸ್-ರೇನಲ್ಲಿ ಮೂಳೆ ಮುರಿತ ಅಥವಾ ಹೊರಗಿನ ವಸ್ತುಗಳಿಲ್ಲ. ರೋಗಿಯನ್ನು ಪ್ರಾದೇಶಿಕ ಬ್ಲಾಕ್ ಅಡಿಯಲ್ಲಿ ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸಾ ಕೊಠಡಿಗೆ ಕರೆದೊಯ್ಯಲಾಯಿತು. ಗಾಯವನ್ನು ನಾರ್ಮಲ್ ಸಲೈನ್‌ನಿಂದ ಸಂಪೂರ್ಣವಾಗಿ ತೊಳೆಯಲಾಯಿತು. ಕತ್ತರಿಸಿದ ಫ್ಲೆಕ್ಸರ್ ಪಾಲಿಸಿಸ್ ಲಾಂಗಸ್ ಸ್ನಾಯುರಜ್ಜು ಮಾರ್ಪಡಿಸಿದ ಕೆಸ್ಲರ್ ತಂತ್ರವನ್ನು ಬಳಸಿ 3-0 ಹೀರಲಾಗದ ಹೊಲಿಗೆಗಳೊಂದಿಗೆ ದುರಸ್ತಿ ಮಾಡಲಾಯಿತು. ಹೆಬ್ಬೆರಳಿನ ಸ್ನಾಯುಗಳನ್ನು ಶುದ್ಧೀಕರಿಸಲಾಯಿತು ಮತ್ತು ಹತ್ತಿರ ತರಲಾಯಿತು. ಚರ್ಮವನ್ನು 4-0 ಹೀರಬಹುದಾದ ಹೊಲಿಗೆಗಳೊಂದಿಗೆ ಮುಚ್ಚಲಾಯಿತು. ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರದ ಯೋಜನೆಯು ಹೆಬ್ಬೆರಳಿಗೆ ಸ್ಪ್ಲಿಂಟ್, ಬ್ರಾಡ್-ಸ್ಪೆಕ್ಟ್ರಮ್ ಆಂಟಿಬಯೋಟಿಕ್ಸ್ ಮತ್ತು ಒಂದು ವಾರದಲ್ಲಿ ಮೂಳೆಚಿಕಿತ್ಸಾ ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸಾಲಯದಲ್ಲಿ ಮುಂದಿನ ತಪಾಸಣೆಯನ್ನು ಒಳಗೊಂಡಿದೆ. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Nagaraja]] ಅವರಿಗೆ ಕಾರ್ಯವಿಧಾನ ಮತ್ತು ಶಸ್ತ್ರಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆಯ ನಂತರದ ಆರೈಕೆಯ ಸೂಚನೆಗಳನ್ನು ತಿಳಿಸಲಾಯಿತು.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-KRM-2024-0042]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-KRM-2024-0042]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mandya District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|General-A]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi Kumar]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Hegde]]
Procedure / findings: Patient is a 22-year-old female wood-working machine operator who presented with a traumatic injury to her left hand sustained at her workplace three days prior. She reports a deep cut while operating a table saw. On examination, there is a 4cm linear laceration over the thenar eminence with underlying tendon exposure. The flexor pollicis longus tendon appears partially transected. No signs of infection. X-ray of the hand shows no fractures or foreign bodies. The patient was taken to the Operating Theatre under regional block. The wound was thoroughly irrigated with normal saline. The lacerated flexor pollicis longus tendon was repaired using a modified Kessler technique with 3-0 non-absorbable sutures. The thenar musculature was debrided and approximated. The skin was closed with 4-0 absorbable sutures. Post-operative plan includes a splint for the thumb, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and a follow-up in the orthopedic clinic in one week. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Nagaraja]] was informed of the procedure and post-operative care instructions.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-KRM-2024-0042]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-KRM-2024-0042]]

payload.persona: Pandurang Godekar, a 49‑year‑old farmer who blends curiosity with practical ‘jugaad’, they worry about market swings yet tirelessly work to modernise their fields while keeping village traditions alive.
payload.cultural_background: Pandurang comes from a traditional agrarian community in Gadag, a historic town in northern Karnataka. His family follows Kannada customs, celebrating Ugadi, Sankranti, and local temple festivals with folk dances like Yakshagana and devotional music. Being a Hindu in a rural setting, he participates in community rituals at the village shrine and observes seasonal harvest ceremonies. The regional culture also values practical ingenuity - the age-old 'jugaad' mindset that influences his farming practices.

He cannot read or write in any language, but his decades of market interaction have given him functional spoken English and Hindi, enabling him to negotiate prices, understand basic instructions, and follow radio programmes about agriculture. He relies on oral tradition and memory, often using folk sayings to convey complex ideas. His linguistic repertoire reflects a pragmatic blend of regional, national, and market languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Pandurang possesses deep practical knowledge of rain-fed and irrigated crop production, especially millet, pulses, and oilseeds native to Karnataka. He is adept at animal husbandry, managing cattle and goats for milk and draught power. Over the years he has honed market-oriented skills such as price negotiation, product grading, and leveraging local traders to sell produce. He can maintain basic farm machinery, repair bullock-drawn ploughs, and apply low-cost pest control using neem and trap crops. His expertise also includes seasonal planning based on monsoon forecasts and maintaining seed banks of traditional varieties.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]

  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Gadag.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]

- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

ರೋಗನಿರ್ಣಯ: ಸಮುದಾಯ-ಪಡೆದ ನ್ಯುಮೋನಿಯಾ, ಬಲ ಕೆಳಗಿನ ಲೋಬ್.

ಚಿಕಿತ್ಸೆ: 7 ದಿನಗಳಿಗೆ ದಿನಕ್ಕೆ ಮೂರು ಬಾರಿ 500mg ಮೌಖಿಕ ಅಮೋಕ್ಸಿಸಿಲಿನ್ ಪ್ರಾರಂಭಿಸಲಾಗಿದೆ, ಜ್ವರಕ್ಕಾಗಿ ಪ್ಯಾರಸಿಟಮಾಲ್ ಮತ್ತು ವಿಶ್ರಾಂತಿಗೆ ಸಲಹೆ ನೀಡಲಾಗಿದೆ. 3 ದಿನಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಫಾಲೋ-ಅಪ್.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Gadag]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-GA-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Shankaragouda Patil]] | DOB [[DOB|1975-06-15]] | Age: [[AGE|49]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ramesh Kumar]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Siddarama Patil]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4421]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Gadag]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough with fever for 5 days, associated with chest discomfort and difficulty breathing.

History of present illness: Patient reports cough productive of yellowish sputum, low-grade fever up to 100.8°F, retrosternal chest pain worse on deep inspiration, and progressive dyspnea over the past week. Symptoms began after exposure to dusty conditions during field work. No known sick contacts.

Physical examination: Temperature 100.6°F, pulse 98/min, respiratory rate 22/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in right lower zone with occasional crackles. No wheezing.

Investigations: CBC shows leukocytosis with neutrophilia. Chest X-ray reveals right lower lobe consolidation. Sputum Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters.

Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia, right lower lobe.

Treatment: Started on oral amoxicillin 500mg TID for 7 days, paracetamol for fever, and advised rest. Follow-up in 3 days.

Advice: Complete course of antibiotics, increase fluid intake, avoid dusty environments, and return immediately if breathing difficulty worsens.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags

  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;

GOOD:

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SMHS Hospital Srinagar]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] [[AGE|28]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Arrived by ambulance [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|JK01AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Shabir Ahmad]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419234567]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient presents with severe abdominal pain, weight loss of 15kg over 3 months, intermittent fever, and fatigue. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 95 bpm, temperature 38.2°C, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Abdomen tender in epigastric region with guarding. Patient appears cachectic and dehydrated.

Nursing assessment: Patient reports worsening pain over past 48 hours, unable to eat solid food, taking only liquids. Reports occasional nausea and vomiting. No known allergies. Previous medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 2 years ago, hypertension for 1 year. Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice daily, Amlodipine 5mg once daily. Family history positive for father with gastric cancer at age 55. Patient smokes 10 cigarettes daily, drinks alcohol occasionally.

Oncology workup initiated: CBC shows normocytic anemia with Hb 8.2 g/dL, WBC 6.5×10^9/L, platelets 180×10^9/L. Liver function tests reveal elevated ALT and AST. Serum tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) pending. Abdominal ultrasound shows gastric wall thickening with possible lymphadenopathy. CT scan ordered for staging. Patient educated about need for endoscopy and biopsy. Pain management with IV analgesics initiated. Nutritional support consulted. Patient and family counseled about potential diagnosis of gastric carcinoma given family history and presentation.

payload.cultural_background: Sha Shamar hails from the vibrant Kashmiri Muslim community of Srinagar, an urban centre in the Kashmir Valley. The city’s rich heritage of wooden handicrafts, papier‑mâché art, and Pashmina weaving forms part of his everyday surroundings. Growing up in a close‑knit neighbourhood, he has been exposed to a blend of modern urban life and the age‑old customs that celebrate hospitality, community gatherings and respect for nature.
payload.linguistic_background: He is fluent in Kashmiri, both spoken and written, comfortable with the Perso‑Arabic script used for the language. Through his schooling, he has functional proficiency in English – can understand textbooks, converse at a basic level and write simple essays. He has limited exposure to Hindi and Urdu, mainly through media, but does not consider them as fluent languages.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Sha shows a natural flair for visual arts, especially sketching and digital photography. He is proficient with basic computer operations and uses graphic design tools like Canva and Photoshop for creating social media content. His command of Kashmiri calligraphy allows him to produce stylised text for local newsletters. Additionally, he has a good understanding of Kashmiri folk crafts, enabling him to assist family members in Pashmina maintenance and craft‑selling decisions. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 28
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Keypunch Operator
- education_level: Technical diploma or certificate not equal to degree
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: Jammer Shamar is a 28-year-old male keypunch operator with a technical diploma, currently married, working in an urban setting in Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir. He is fluent in Kashmiri, which he reads and writes in the Perso-Arabic script, and has functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes a quiet love for poetry, Sufi music, and mountain sketching, while managing anxiety and occasional impulse art-supply purchases.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]

- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Srinagar District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SDH-SR-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Jammer Shamar]] | DOB [[DOB|1996-05-15]] | Age: [[AGE|28]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Keypunch Operator]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riyaz Ahmad]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Amina Shamar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419876543]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4421]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Srinagar]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with weight loss and low-grade fever

History of present illness: Patient reports productive cough with yellowish sputum, intermittent fever in the evenings, and progressive weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the past month. He also complains of mild chest discomfort and night sweats. Past medical history reveals well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 2 years ago.

Examination: General condition - moderately built, conscious, oriented. Vital signs - BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 92/min, Temperature 37.8°C, Respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 94% on room air. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds in right upper lobe with occasional crackles. Abdominal examination shows mild hepatomegaly.

Investigations: Sputum for AFB - positive, HbA1c - 7.2%, Chest X-ray - right upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation.

Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus

Treatment: Started on DOTS regimen - Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol. Diabetes management with Metformin 500mg twice daily. Nutritional counseling provided.

Follow-up: Next appointment in 2 weeks for DOTS administration and blood sugar monitoring. Patient advised to complete full course of anti-tubercular therapy.
