Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Karnataka, district=Mandya.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Karnataka
- district: Mandya
- first_language: Kannada

Her primary language is Kannada, and she works in a small workshop, operating CNC routers and band saws. She is curious about technology and enjoys folk music and community gatherings.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):

Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]

Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Mandya]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-MND-01]]

Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Wood Working Machine Operatives, Other]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Kumar]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Mandya]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with evening fever and weight loss

History of present illness: Patient reports progressive cough with yellowish sputum for 3 weeks. Associated with low-grade evening fever and loss of appetite. No hemoptysis reported. Patient works in wood workshop with poor ventilation.

Examination: General condition: Thin, conscious, cooperative. Vitals: BP 110/70 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temp 99.2°F, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Chest: Decreased air entry in left lower zone, bronchial breath sounds present. No clubbing.

Investigations: Sputum for AFB: Positive (2+). Chest X-ray: Left lower lobe consolidation with cavitation. CBC: Hb 10.2 g/dL, TLC 8500/cu mm. ESR: 65 mm/hr.

Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis (New case) - Left lower lobe

Treatment: Started on Category I DOTS regimen:
- Isoniazid 300mg daily
- Rifampicin 450mg daily  
- Pyrazinamide 1200mg daily
- Ethambutol 750mg daily

Counselling: Patient educated about TB treatment importance, side effects monitoring, and infection control measures at workplace.

Follow-up: Review in 2 weeks for treatment response and side effects monitoring.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Madhya Pradesh, district=Mandsaur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Teacher, Infant School Pre-Primary Education Teaching Associate Professionals, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Urban
- state: Madhya Pradesh

payload.sports_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who enjoys early morning walks through her vegetable garden and participates in casual cricket games at the village ground, cheerfully follows the Maharashtra women’s cricket team and joins a local women’s volleyball club for weekly fitness.
payload.arts_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, an enthusiastic creator of abstract folk motifs on fabric and paper, spends evenings immersed in the ragas of Pandit Bhimsen Joshi and the tabla rhythms of Ustad Zakir Hussain, while practicing lively Lavani steps during village celebrations, and draws inspiration from the vivid colors of local Kalamkari artisans.
payload.travel_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who prefers spontaneous, budget‑friendly getaways, has explored the misty hills of Mahabaleshwar, the sandy beaches of Goa, and the ancient forts of Raigad; they often travel with a small group of friends during festival holidays to combine cultural immersion with relaxed sightseeing.
payload.culinary_persona: Lalita Tirvadekar, who delights in the sweet aroma of fresh coconut in dishes like sol kadhi, loves the tangy heat of misal pav and the festive sweetness of modak, often prepares simple vangi bhaji at home using brinjal and tamarind, yet prefers to savor street‑food specialties such as vada pav and bhel puri during village fairs.
payload.persona: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old mono‑line operator who weaves technical precision with vibrant folk art, thrives on community rituals yet juggles anxiety‑driven moods, lives on disciplined savings but occasionally splurges on colorful craft supplies; they balance imagination with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Lalita Tirvadekar comes from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu community that settled in the rural hinterland of Jalgaon, Maharashtra. Her family maintains a blend of coastal Konkani customs—such as devotion to Ganesh and sharing of coconut‑based festive foods—and the agrarian traditions of interior Maharashtra, like local fairs (shashti), collective farming rituals and village temple gatherings. This cultural mix shapes her worldview, fostering a respect for community harmony and a love for folk arts.
payload.linguistic_background: Lalita grew up speaking Konkani at home and Marathi in the village and school setting. Though she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language, she has strong oral communication skills. She can understand spoken Hindi due to everyday interactions and can follow work instructions in Konkani and Marathi. Her fluency lets her convey ideas creatively through storytelling and oral explanations, which aligns with her imaginative nature.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lalita works as a mono line operator, handling installation, maintenance, and troubleshooting of single‑wire telephone lines in her rural area. She possesses manual dexterity, an ear for line quality, and the ability to interpret oral work instructions. Her job requires clear communication with villagers, so she has strong interpersonal and conflict‑resolution skills. Additionally, her creative side finds expression in folk art, embroidery, and improvisational storytelling, which she often uses to convey information in a memorable way.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['mono line operation', 'basic electrical troubleshooting', 'manual dexterity', 'oral instruction following', 'community liaison', 'folk art creation', 'embroidery', 'conflict mediation']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lalita enjoys creating abstract paintings and colorful folk motifs on fabric and paper. She spends evenings listening to classical Hindustani music and practicing the lively Lavani dance steps during village celebrations. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables and marigolds, gives her a sense of calm. She loves oral storytelling, sharing local legends with children, and attending weekly village fairs where she explores new crafts and ideas.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['abstract painting', 'folk motif creation', 'listening to Hindustani classical music', 'practicing Lavani dance', 'gardening', 'oral storytelling', 'attending village fairs', 'exploring new crafts']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lalita aspires to advance from being a mono line operator to a supervisory role within the telecom department, where she can mentor other workers. She plans to enroll in a government adult‑literacy program to gain basic reading and writing skills, which will enable her to handle records independently. In the longer term, she dreams of turning her talent for folk art and embroidery into a small home‑based handicraft business, providing supplemental income for her family and promoting cultural heritage.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from Maharashtra’s Jalgaon district, currently married, with a Konkani first language and limited formal education. She works as a mono‑operator in a rural telecom setting, demonstrating strong manual skills and community engagement despite anxiety‑driven mood swings. Her lifestyle includes disciplined savings, occasional splurges on folk art, and a preference for oral communication over written literacy.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Jalgaon.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Konkani.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Mono Operator
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Jalgaon
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old female from Maharashtra’s Jalgaon district, currently married, with a Konkani first language and limited formal education. She works as a mono‑operator in a rural telecom setting, demonstrating strong manual skills and community engagement despite anxiety‑driven mood swings. Her lifestyle includes disciplined savings, occasional splurges on folk art, and a preference for oral communication over written literacy.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Chief complaint: Patient presents with recurrent headaches, palpitations, and anxiety episodes for the past 2 months.

History of present illness: Mrs. Lalita Tirvadekar, a 34-year-old female mono operator from rural Jalgaon, reports experiencing throbbing headaches typically in the morning, associated with palpitations and excessive sweating. She describes feeling anxious and restless, particularly during work hours. Symptoms are exacerbated by stress and improve with rest. No fever, vomiting, or visual disturbances reported.

- Pulse: 98/min, regular

- CNS: No focal deficits, mild photophobia

Investigations:

- Lipid profile: Within normal limits
- ECG: Sinus tachycardia, no ischemic changes

Diagnosis:

2. Anxiety disorder
3. Stress-related headaches

Treatment plan:

- Prescribe anxiolytic: Alprazolam 0.25mg as needed for anxiety

Instructions: Take medications as prescribed, avoid stressful situations, maintain adequate hydration. Return immediately if severe headache, chest pain, or neurological symptoms develop.

payload.persona: Lalita Tirvadekar is a 34‑year‑old mono‑line operator who weaves technical precision with vibrant folk art, thrives on community rituals yet juggles anxiety‑driven moods, lives on disciplined savings but occasionally splurges on colorful craft supplies; they balance imagination with a relaxed, sometimes disorganized lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Lalita Tirvadekar comes from a Konkani‑speaking Hindu community that settled in the rural hinterland of Jalgaon, Maharashtra. Her family maintains a blend of coastal Konkani customs—such as devotion to Ganesh and sharing of coconut‑based festive foods—and the agrarian traditions of interior Maharashtra, like local fairs (shashti), collective farming rituals and village temple gatherings. This cultural mix shapes her worldview, fostering a respect for community harmony and a love for folk arts.
payload.linguistic_background: Lalita grew up speaking Konkani at home and Marathi in the village and school setting. Though she is illiterate and cannot read or write in any language, she has strong oral communication skills. She can understand spoken Hindi due to everyday interactions and can follow work instructions in Konkani and Marathi. Her fluency lets her convey ideas creatively through storytelling and oral explanations, which aligns with her imaginative nature.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lalita works as a mono line operator, handling installation, maintenance, and troubleshooting of single‑wire telephone lines in her rural area. She possesses manual dexterity, an ear for line quality, and the ability to interpret oral work instructions. Her job requires clear communication with villagers, so she has strong interpersonal and conflict‑resolution skills. Additionally, her creative side finds expression in folk art, embroidery, and improvisational storytelling, which she often uses to convey information in a memorable way.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lalita enjoys creating abstract paintings and colorful folk motifs on fabric and paper. She spends evenings listening to classical Hindustani music and practicing the lively Lavani dance steps during village celebrations. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables and marigolds, gives her a sense of calm. She loves oral storytelling, sharing local legends with children, and attending weekly village fairs where she explores new crafts and ideas.

payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lalita aspires to advance from being a mono line operator to a supervisory role within the telecom department, where she can mentor other workers. payload.system_prompt:

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

payload.persona: Devanti Kumari is a diligent 23‑year‑old jewellery supervisor whose structured work ethic and vibrant social spirit are tempered by frequent anxiety and a dream of leading her own craft studio.
payload.cultural_background: Devanti comes from a Maithili‑speaking family in the rural hinterland of Saharsa, Bihar. Growing up in a joint‑family setting, she learned the importance of mutual support, hospitality and communal gatherings.
payload.linguistic_background: Devanti is a native speaker of Maithili and uses it as her primary means of communication at home and in the community. Although she cannot read or write due to her illiteracy, she has acquired basic comprehension of spoken Hindi through daily interactions and media such as radio. She does not have proficiency in English or any other language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Devanti has developed strong supervisory skills in the jewellery and precision metal‑working sector. She coordinates daily production schedules, ensures that engraving patterns meet design specifications, and conducts quality checks on finished pieces. Her hands‑on experience with tools such as hand‑drills, gravers and polishing machines gives her practical expertise in metal shaping and fine detailing. Additionally, she manages a small team of artisans, handling labour allocation, conflict resolution and on‑the‑spot training. Her keen eye for market trends helps her adapt designs to customer preferences in the local market.

Cooking regional dishes such as litti‑chokha and preparing sweets for festivals is a cherished pastime. She also relaxes by listening to the village radio, playing carrom with friends, and practising simple breathing exercises to manage her occasional anxiety.

payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Devanti aspires to open her own modest jewellery and engraving studio within the next three to five years, allowing her to generate a stable income for her family and create employment opportunities for other women in her village. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Thrissur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 56
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Kerala
- district: Thrissur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 56-year-old female from Thrissur, Kerala, who is currently married and retired, has primary education and speaks Malayalam. She lives in an urban zone and is a homemaker. Clinically, she has a disciplined lifestyle with a modest budget, occasional financial disorganization, and a history of postponing bills, while also engaging in informal counseling and basic first-aid. Her cultural background is Syrian Christian, and she enjoys low-key family trips and traditional Kerala cooking.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Hazaribagh.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 65
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Hazaribagh
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 65-year-old female from Jharkhand, Hazaribagh, who is widowed and retired, speaks Maithili at home and has limited formal education. She lives in a rural area and manages household responsibilities, including traditional cooking and community caretaking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional participation in local senior walking groups.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
शल्यक्रिया नोट — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|मैथिली जनरल हॉस्पिटल]] भर्ती [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] वार्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|राम सिंह]] [[AGE|65]] [[GENDER|Female]] सर्जन [[DOCTOR_NAME|डॉ. अंजलि देवी]]
प्रक्रिया / निष्कर्ष: मरीजक दाहिना ऊपरी चतुर्थांश दर्द आ पीलियाक शिकायत छल। शल्यक्रिया-पूर्व अल्ट्रासाउंडमे बहुत रास पित्तक पथरी संग पित्तशयशोथ देखौलक। मरीजक सामान्य एनेस्थीसियाक अंतर्गत लैप्रोस्कोपिक कोलेसिस्टेक्टोमी कयल गेल। शल्यक्रियाक दौरानक निष्कर्षमे एकटा दीर्घकालिक सूजन बला पित्तशय संग बहुत रास पथरी देखौलक। सिस्टिक डक्ट आ आर्टरीक पहचान कयल गेल आ बान्हल गेल। पित्तशयकेँ यकृतक तलसँ अलग कऽ 10mm पोर्टक माध्यमसँ निकालल गेल। रक्तस्तंभन प्राप्त कयल गेल। कोनो पित्त नलीक चोट नहि देखल गेल। शल्यक्रिया-पश्चातक अवधि सामान्य रहल। मरीजकेँ अवलोकनक लेल सर्जिकल वार्डमे स्थानांतरित कयल गेल।

[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|सीता देवी]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-78945]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Hazaribagh Medical College Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Devi]] [[AGE|65]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with right upper quadrant pain and jaundice. Pre-operative ultrasound revealed multiple gallstones with cholecystitis. Patient underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. Intraoperative findings showed a chronically inflamed gallbladder with multiple calculi. Cystic duct and artery were identified and ligated. Gallbladder was dissected from the liver bed and removed through a 10mm port. Hemostasis was achieved. No bile duct injury noted. Post-operative course was uneventful. Patient transferred to surgical ward for observation.

[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Prasad]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-78945]]

payload.professional_persona: Zainab Khatun is an experienced community caretaker who manages a large rural household, prepares traditional local cuisine, weaves shawls, and mediates neighbourhood disputes, and they apply their practical mindset, compassionate nature and balanced curiosity to innovate within folk traditions.
payload.linguistic_persona: Zainab Khatun communicates fluently in the regional language of their village, can converse in the local Hindi dialect, and memorises Arabic verses for prayers, though they cannot read or write in any script.
payload.sports_persona: Zainab Khatun enjoys watching Jamshedpur FC football matches on TV, attends local kabaddi tournaments, and maintains personal fitness through daily morning walks and gentle yoga, and they occasionally join a senior women's walking club that meets at the village school ground.
payload.arts_persona: Zainab Khatun immerses themselves in the regional folk song repertoire, especially the soulful renditions of the late Lalu Mishra, relishes the poetry of local bard Bhola Bhiya, and delights in classic Hindi cinema songs of Lata Mangeshkar and Kishore Kumar, and they often hum these tunes while weaving shawls.
payload.travel_persona: Zainab Khatun prefers short, purpose-driven trips such as travelling to the nearest town market in Hazaribagh for fresh produce, visiting relatives in Ranchi during festive seasons, and occasionally joining organized pilgrim tours to the Shravan Temple in Deoghar, and they balance modest travel budgets with a love for family reunions.
payload.culinary_persona: Zainab Khatun excels at preparing traditional local dishes such as mutton keema, spicy dal with mustard seed, sewai sweet flavored with cardamom and saffron, and seasonal vegetable bhaji from their kitchen garden, preferring fresh, locally sourced ingredients while occasionally experimenting with a pinch of modern spices, and they take great pride in serving these meals to family and guests.
payload.persona: Zainab Khatun combines a lifelong devotion to community caretaking with a pragmatic curiosity, weaving stories and shawls while juggling budgeting, health rituals, and occasional sweet indulgences, and they are guided by a steady, compassionate temperament.
payload.cultural_background: Zainab Khatun belongs to a Maithili‑speaking Muslim community that has lived for generations in the rural hinterland of Hazaribagh, Jharkhand. The community blends the linguistic and folkloric traditions of the Maithili culture—such as panche dhun folk songs, seasonal fairs, and oral storytelling—with Islamic religious practices like daily prayers, Ramadan fasting, and Eid celebrations. Village life is organised around joint families, agricultural cycles, and a strong sense of neighbourly reciprocity, which have shaped Zainab’s worldview and daily routine.
payload.linguistic_background: Zainab grew up speaking Maithili as her mother tongue and uses it as her primary mode of communication at home and in the village. She can understand and converse in spoken Hindi, especially the local dialect, but due to her lack of formal schooling she cannot read or write in any script. For religious purposes she has learned to recite Arabic verses from memory and can follow the Urdu script of the Qur'an with the help of family members. Her oral proficiency enables her to preserve and transmit folk stories and culinary knowledge across generations.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Zainab’s expertise lies in managing a large rural household, preparing traditional Maithili Muslim cuisine, preserving oral folklore, providing informal caregiving and community support, performing basic arithmetic for budgeting, weaving handicrafts, and mediating disputes within the neighbourhood. Her practical mindset and compassionate nature allow her to apply these skills with reliability and flexibility.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Maithili Muslim cooking", "Oral folklore preservation", "Community caregiving", "Basic arithmetic budgeting", "Handicraft weaving", "Neighbourhood dispute mediation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Zainab enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, weaving colorful shawls, tending a small kitchen garden of seasonal vegetables, reciting du’a and Qur’anic verses from memory, sharing stories with grandchildren, and participating in women’s self‑help groups that discuss health, nutrition, and religious topics.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk music", "Weaving colorful shawls", "Tending a kitchen garden", "Reciting du'a and Qur'anic verses from memory", "Storytelling with grandchildren", "Participating in women’s self‑help groups"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 65, Zainab aspires to remain an active pillar of her community. She wishes to mentor younger women in traditional cooking and weaving, document oral stories for future generations, and organize health and hygiene awareness camps for the villagers. She also hopes to support her grandchildren’s education through informal tutoring and to continue her voluntary work at the mosque, ensuring that the values of compassion and harmony are passed on.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 65-year-old female from Hazaribagh, Jharkhand, who is widowed and retired, speaks Maithili at home and has limited formal education. She lives in a rural setting and manages household responsibilities, including traditional cooking and community caretaking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional participation in local women's walking groups.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jharkhand, district=Hazaribagh.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 65
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Hazaribagh
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: A 65-year-old female from Hazaribagh, Jharkhand, who is widowed and retired, speaks Maithili at home and has limited formal education. She lives in a rural setting and manages household responsibilities, including traditional cooking and community caretaking. Her lifestyle includes daily walks and occasional participation in local women's walking groups.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

अगिला भेट 3 दिनमे [[HOSPITAL_NAME|अस्पताल]], नेत्र विज्ञान विभाग, कमरा 204 मे निश्चित कयल गेल अछि।

payload.professional_persona: Devi W S, an experienced homemaker and cultural custodian, they blend their mastery of traditional regional cooking (including Eromba, Singju, and Champhaa), handloom weaving, and ritual organization to manage family events, mentor younger women, and preserve heritage, combining meticulous planning with an earnest curiosity for new ideas.
payload.linguistic_persona: Devi W S, fluent in their native tongue for storytelling, daily conversation, and cultural rituals, they also use basic English when needed, relying on oral memory as they are illiterate in both scripts.
payload.sports_persona: Devi W S, they enjoy gentle morning walks in the neighbourhood park, follow NEROCA FC’s football matches on television, and occasionally join a senior yoga circle, preferring low‑impact activity that fits their routine.
payload.arts_persona: Devi W S, they cherish traditional folk songs and classical pakhawaj and pena performances, often listening to maestro Guru Thoiba Singh and vocalist Lila Devi, while also attending community talks on regional art and weaving intricate shawls inspired by these cultural expressions.
payload.travel_persona: Devi W S, they prefer short trips to nearby cultural sites such as Kangla Fort and Loktak Lake, occasional pilgrimages to Puri’s Jagannath Temple, and leisurely visits to Shillong’s tea gardens, planning each journey meticulously while balancing health constraints.
payload.culinary_persona: Devi W S, they excel at cooking Eromba, Singju, and Champhaa, favor fresh coriander, chilies, and seasonal vegetables, and relish sweet rasgulla on festival days.
payload.persona: Devi W S is a curious, organized 79‑year‑old keeper of tradition; despite occasional anxiety and forgetfulness, they lovingly preserve folk stories, recipes, and rituals for their family.
payload.cultural_background: Devi grew up in the urban neighborhoods of Imphal East, Manipur, a culturally vibrant city known for its classical dance forms, handloom traditions, and festivals like Yaoshang and Lai Haraoba. As a Meitei Hindu, her family blended the rich Manipuri heritage with Vaishnavite devotional practices, fostering a deep appreciation for art, ritual, and community cohesion.
payload.linguistic_background: Devi's mother tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she uses fluently for daily conversation, storytelling, and cultural rituals. She also communicates in basic English when needed, especially with younger relatives, but because she never attended school, she remains illiterate in both Manipuri and English scripts. She cannot read or write, relying on oral tradition and memory for information.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Devi excels at traditional Manipuri cooking, preparing dishes such as Eromba, Singju, and Champhaa with authentic flavors. She is adept at handloom weaving, creating intricate handwoven shawls and textiles for family use and occasional community gifting. Her lifelong experience as a homemaker endows her with strong household management, including budgeting, planning family events, and caring for elders. She is a gifted oral storyteller, preserving folk tales, myths, and histories of the Meitei people. Additionally, she is knowledgeable in conducting Hindu rituals, preparing puja items, and guiding family members through religious ceremonies.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Traditional Manipuri cooking', 'Handloom weaving', 'Household management and budgeting', 'Oral storytelling of Meitei folklore', 'Conducting Hindu rituals and puja preparations']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Devi enjoys listening to traditional Manipuri folk songs and classical music, especially the pakhawaj and pena performances. She loves spending time in her small garden, tending to marigolds and seasonal vegetables. Reading is not possible, but she likes to sit with her grandchildren as they read storybooks aloud. She also takes pleasure in preparing elaborate puja arrangements during festivals and occasionally knitting simple shawls. Her curiosity leads her to attend community talks about historical art and to experiment with new recipes.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Manipuri folk music', 'Gardening with seasonal vegetables', 'Spending time with grandchildren', 'Preparing puja decorations for festivals', 'Knitting simple shawls', 'Attending community talks on art and culture']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 79, Devi aims to preserve and transmit Manipuri cultural heritage by teaching her grandchildren traditional recipes, weaving techniques, and folk stories. She wishes to support her family's health and education, especially ensuring her great-grandchildren receive good schooling. She also hopes to volunteer at the local temple and community center, helping organize festivals and assisting younger women in mastering household rituals. Her ambition is to leave a legacy of cultural continuity and familial harmony.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from Imphal East, Manipur, with an illiterate education level, speaks Manipuri as her first language and basic English. She resides in an urban zone and maintains a traditional lifestyle, organizing family rituals and preserving folk stories. Her daily routine includes gentle walks and participation in community yoga, while she enjoys classical music and traditional cuisine.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]

Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:

- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]

- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]

- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

जाँच पर, महत्वपूर्ण संकेत स्थिर अछि: रक्तचाप 110/70 mmHg, नाड़ी 80/min, वजन 55 kg। पेटक जाँचमे एकटा जीवित भ्रूण देखबैत अछि जकर भ्रूणक हृदयक धड़कन 140 bpm अछि। कोनो पीलापन अथवा सूजन नहि देखल गेल, सिवाय दोनू पैरमे हल्का पिटिंग एडिमाक।

योजना: नियमित ANC जारी राखू। दिनमे एकटा टैबलेट आयरन फोलिक एसिड 100mg लिखल गेल अछि। विश्राम करबाक सलाह देल गेल, पैर ऊँच राखू, आ कोनो चेतावनी संकेत जेना कि गंभीर सिरदर्द, रक्तस्राव, अथवा भ्रूणक हलचल कम होयब रिपोर्ट करू। अगला अपॉइंटमेंट 4 सप्ताहमे निर्धारित अछि। आशा कार्यकर्ता [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|आशा कार्यकर्ताक नाम]] घर पर अनुवर्ती कार्रवाई करबाक लेल। मरीजकेँ ANC कार्ड आ IFA टैबलेट देल गेल। संपर्क नंबर [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]। मरीजक [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-BR-2024-001234]] आ [[VOTER_ID|VOTER1234567]] विवरण सत्यापित कयल गेल। मरीजक [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] ओकर स्वास्थ्य रिकॉर्डसँ जोड़ल गेल।
payload.generated_text_en:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Bansgaon]] Village [[VILLAGE|Bansgaon]] District [[DISTRICT|Saharsa]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Devanti Kumari]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]] | ANC Visit 20 weeks

Patient presents for routine antenatal checkup. Last menstrual period 1st April 2024, estimated date of delivery 8th January 2025. Gravida 1, Para 0. Reports mild swelling in lower extremities, denies headaches, visual disturbances, or abdominal pain.

On examination, vitals are stable: Blood Pressure 110/70 mmHg, Pulse 80/min, Weight 55 kg. Abdominal examination reveals a single, live fetus with fetal heart sounds 140 bpm. No pallor or edema noted except mild pitting edema on both feet.

Investigations: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Urine routine and microscopy normal. Blood group O positive.

Diagnosis: Normal singleton pregnancy at 20 weeks gestation. Mild peripheral edema.

Plan: Continue routine ANC. Prescribed one tablet of Iron Folic Acid 100mg daily. Advised to rest, elevate feet, and report any warning signs like severe headache, bleeding, or decreased fetal movements. Next appointment scheduled in 4 weeks. ASHA worker [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sita Devi]] to follow up at home. Patient provided with ANC card and IFA tablets. Contact number [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. Patient's [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-BR-2024-001234]] and [[VOTER_ID|VOTER1234567]] details verified. Patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] linked to her health record.
