संदर्भ [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] कडून [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Hingoli]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patil]]
विषय: [[PATIENT_NAME|Vijay Kumar Shinde]], [[AGE|67]] / [[GENDER|Male]], जिल्हा [[DISTRICT|Hingoli]]
कारण: सतत होणारी छातीत दुखणे आणि श्वास घेण्यास त्रास होणे यांची तपासणी.

आदरणीय सहकारी,

मी [[PATIENT_NAME|Vijay Kumar Shinde]] यांना पुढील तपासणीसाठी संदर्भित करत आहे. रुग्णाकडे दोन आठवड्यांचा इतिहास आहे, ज्यामध्ये मधूनमधून छातीच्या मागील भागात दाबल्यासारखे जाणारी अस्वस्थता होते, जी डाव्या हाताकडे पसरते. हे त्रास विश्रांतीच्या वेळी होतात आणि 10-15 मिनिटे टिकतात. संबंधित लक्षणांमध्ये शारीरिक श्रमानंतर श्वास घेण्यास त्रास होणे आणि सौम्य घाम येणे यांचा समावेश आहे.

[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] मधून 15 वर्षांपासून उच्च रक्तदाबाचा इतिहास दिसून येतो, सध्या अनियमित औषधोपचार सुरू आहेत. आजचे रक्तदाब 150/90 mmHg आहे. ईसीजीमध्ये V4-V6 लीड्समध्ये ST सेगमेंट डिप्रेशन दिसते. ट्रोपोनिन नकारात्मक आहे.

रुग्ण [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Shinde]] यांचे पती आहेत, मर्यादित शिक्षण असूनही कॉल सेंटर ऑपरेटर म्हणून काम करतात. [[VILLAGE|Khadki]] मध्ये राहतात, हिंघोळी जिल्ह्यातील. संपर्क: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].

कृपया हृदय तपासणीची व्यवस्था करा, ज्यामध्ये स्ट्रेस टेस्ट आणि इकोकार्डियोग्राफीचा समावेश आहे. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH02 20100012345]] प्रवाससाठी उपलब्ध आहे. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z123456789]] ओळखीसाठी.

धन्यवाद,
डॉ. राजेश पाटील
एमबीबीएस, एमडी (सामान्य औषधोपचार)
सिव्हिल हॉस्पिटल हिंघोळी

payload.generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Hingoli]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patil]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Vijay Kumar Shinde]], [[AGE|67]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Hingoli]]
Reason: Evaluation of persistent chest pain and shortness of breath

Dear Colleague,

I am referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Vijay Kumar Shinde]] for further evaluation. The patient presents with a 2-week history of intermittent retrosternal chest discomfort described as pressure, radiating to the left arm. Episodes occur at rest and last 10-15 minutes. Associated symptoms include dyspnea on exertion and mild diaphoresis.

[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] reveals a history of hypertension for 15 years, currently on irregular medication. Blood pressure today is 150/90 mmHg. ECG shows ST segment depression in leads V4-V6. Troponin is negative.

Patient is [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Shinde]]'s husband, works as a call centre operator despite limited education. Lives in [[VILLAGE|Khadki]], Hingoli district. Contact: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]].

Please arrange cardiac evaluation including stress test and echocardiography. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|MH02 20100012345]] available for travel. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z123456789]] for identification.

Thank you,
Dr. Rajesh Patil
MBBS, MD (General Medicine)
Civil Hospital Hingoli
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-MH-081]]

payload.sports_persona: Shibani Das, a casual sports enthusiast, enjoys early morning jogs along the Brahmaputra riverbank, follows the IPL fervently cheering for the Delhi Capitals, supports the Assam football team in local league matches, and occasionally joins a weekend yoga class at the community centre.
payload.arts_persona: Shibani Das, an avid listener of Assamese folk tunes, especially the soulful voice of Zubeen Garg and the energetic Bihu drums, often practices Bihu dance routines, delights in short stories by Mamoni Raisom Goswami, and occasionally watches Hindi cinema featuring stars like Ranveer Singh, which fuels their creative spirit.
payload.travel_persona: Shibani Das, a spontaneous traveler who loves day‑trips to Kaziranga National Park for elephant sightings and weekend getaways to Shillong for its cool climate, prefers budgeting travel through train tickets and shared buses, but dreams of a future backpacking tour of Southeast Asia, especially Thailand, while balancing family expectations.
payload.culinary_persona: Shibani Das, a proficient home chef, delights in preparing Assamese specialties like pitha, tenga, masor tenga, and bamboo shoot pickle, often seasoning with mustard oil, khar, and fresh herbs like coriander and mint, while occasionally exploring Hindi street food such as aloo chaat during weekend market visits.
payload.persona: Shibani Das, a 22‑year‑old dreamer who juggles school, a budding catering business, and Bihu festivities with disciplined budgeting, occasional midnight cravings for spicy snacks, and a habit of replaying folk tunes while stirring a pot.
payload.cultural_background: Shibani Das belongs to the Assamese community of northeastern India. Growing up in the urban town of Tinsukia, she has been immersed in the region's tea‑garden culture, the vibrant celebrations of Bihu, and the everyday rhythms of a Hindu household that blends traditional rituals with modern aspirations.
payload.linguistic_background: Shibani's mother tongue is Assamese, which she reads and writes fluently in the Assamese script. She also has functional proficiency in Hindi, both spoken and written, having learned it through formal schooling and daily communication. Her primary education gave her basic literacy in both languages, while her exposure to English is limited to occasional interactions and media consumption.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Shibani is proficient in managing a household, including meal planning, traditional Assamese cooking, budgeting, and childcare. She has strong practical skills in preparing festive dishes such as pitha, tenga, and various fish preparations. She also handles basic digital tasks like using a smartphone for communication, online grocery shopping, and staying connected with family through messaging apps. Additionally, she assists in organizing community events, especially during Bihu, and has basic knowledge of Assamese folk dance.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household Management","Traditional Assamese Cooking","Budgeting","Childcare","Smartphone Use","Community Event Coordination","Basic Assamese Folk Dance"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Shibani enjoys listening to Assamese folk music, dancing Bihu, experimenting with new recipes, and reading short stories in the Assamese language. She likes gardening, especially growing herbs and small vegetables for kitchen use. On weekends, she often visits local markets, watches Hindi movies, and spends time with friends at tea stalls, balancing social interaction with quiet moments of reading or knitting.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Assamese Folk Music","Bihu Dance","Cooking and Recipe Experimentation","Reading Assamese Short Stories","Gardening (Herbs & Vegetables)","Visiting Local Markets","Watching Hindi Movies","Knitting"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Shibani aims to further her education by completing her secondary schooling while simultaneously launching a small home‑based catering venture that offers authentic Assamese festive meals. She hopes to become a recognized community organizer for cultural events, especially Bihu celebrations, and mentor other young women in her neighbourhood to pursue education and self‑reliance. Ultimately, she envisions her family’s financial stability improving through her culinary business, enabling her to support her husband’s aspirations and provide quality education for future children.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 22-year-old female currently married homemaker with primary education, residing in urban Tinsukia, Assam, who speaks Assamese as her first language and has functional Hindi proficiency. She manages household responsibilities while pursuing a budding catering business, with lifestyle cues including midnight cravings for spicy snacks and a habit of listening to Assamese folk music. Her cultural background is Assamese, and she is actively involved in community Bihu festivities.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Tinsukia.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Assamese.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 22
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Urban
- state: Assam
- district: Tinsukia
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 22-year-old female currently married homemaker with primary education, residing in urban Tinsukia, Assam, who speaks Assamese as her first language and has functional Hindi proficiency. She manages household responsibilities while pursuing a budding catering business, with lifestyle cues including midnight cravings for spicy snacks and a habit of listening to Assamese folk music. Her cultural background is Assamese, and she is actively involved in community Bihu festivities.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):

MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]

Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Date: 15 August 2024
Sample Collection: 14 August 2024, 08:30 AM

Patient Details:
22-year-old female homemaker from urban Tinsukia, Assam, presenting with complaints of fatigue and mild abdominal discomfort for the past two weeks. No known chronic illnesses. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol consumption.

Investigations Ordered:
1. Complete Blood Count (CBC)
2. Liver Function Tests (LFT)
3. Kidney Function Tests (KFT)
4. Fasting Blood Glucose (FBG)
5. Lipid Profile

Results:
CBC:
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL (Normal: 12.0-15.5 g/dL)
- Total WBC Count: 7,800/cmm (Normal: 4,000-11,000/cmm)
- Neutrophils: 55%
- Lymphocytes: 35%
- Monocytes: 8%
- Eosinophils: 2%
- Platelets: 2.1 lakhs/cmm (Normal: 1.5-4.5 lakhs/cmm)

LFT:
- Total Bilirubin: 1.2 mg/dL (Normal: 0.3-1.0 mg/dL)
- Direct Bilirubin: 0.4 mg/dL
- SGOT: 45 U/L (Normal: 7-55 U/L)
- SGPT: 52 U/L (Normal: 7-55 U/L)
- Alkaline Phosphatase: 85 U/L (Normal: 30-120 U/L)
- Total Protein: 7.1 g/dL (Normal: 6.4-8.3 g/dL)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL (Normal: 3.5-5.0 g/dL)

KFT:
- Serum Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL (Normal: 0.6-1.1 mg/dL)
- Blood Urea: 18 mg/dL (Normal: 7-20 mg/dL)
- Electrolytes: Within normal limits

Fasting Blood Glucose: 89 mg/dL (Normal: <100 mg/dL)
Lipid Profile:
- Total Cholesterol: 165 mg/dL (Normal: <200 mg/dL)
- LDL: 95 mg/dL (Normal: <100 mg/dL)

- Triglycerides: 110 mg/dL (Normal: <150 mg/dL)

Interpretation:
Mild normocytic anemia with borderline elevated liver enzymes. No significant renal dysfunction. Fasting glucose and lipid profile within normal limits.

Recommendation:
1. Repeat CBC after 2 weeks
2. Ultrasound abdomen if symptoms persist
3. Iron supplementation as per physician's discretion
4. Dietary modifications and adequate hydration
5. Follow-up with Dr. Pranab Kalita in 1 week

payload.arts_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a devoted listener of Marathi Lavani, devotional bhajans and classic Hindi films, often hums melodies of Lata Mangeshkar and Sudhir Phadke while knitting, and appreciates the theatrical performances of local Natak mandalis.

payload.persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a meticulous 64‑year‑old matriarch who blends disciplined budgeting, traditional cooking and community leadership, yet battles chronic worries and occasional splurges on festival indulgences.
payload.cultural_background: Kaushalyabai belongs to a traditional Marathi‑speaking family settled in Jalna, Maharashtra, a region where Marathi culture intermingles with Khandeshi folk traditions. She was raised in a joint family that values hierarchical respect, community celebrations, and the observance of regional festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali, and the Khandeshi Jatra. Even though Jalna is an urban setting, her upbringing is steeped in the conventional Maharashtrian ethos of hospitality, disciplined routine, and reverence for elders.
payload.linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Kaushalyabai cannot read or write in any script, but she is fluent in spoken Marathi, her mother tongue, and Khandeshi, the regional dialect she uses with neighbours and relatives. She also understands basic Hindi from television and community interactions, though she relies on oral communication. Her linguistic skills are practical and centred on daily household interactions, religious rituals, and the organization of community events, where she excels in giving clear verbal instructions and remembering intricate details through mental notes.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kaushalyabai has mastered a suite of domestic and community skills through years of hands‑on experience. She excels in preparing authentic Maharashtrian dishes such as poha, bhakri, and shrikhand, maintaining strict hygienic standards in the kitchen. She manages the household budget meticulously, using mental arithmetic and simple ledger marks despite being illiterate. Her organizational talent shines in planning family events and temple festivals, where she coordinates volunteers, prepares timelines, and ensures every ritual is performed on schedule. Additionally, she is skilled in traditional needlework and embroidery, crafts herbal remedies for common ailments, and acts as a trusted mediator in neighbourhood disputes, leveraging her assertive yet compassionate demeanor.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Kaushalyabai enjoys gardening, especially tending to marigold and neem plants, knitting simple woolen shawls, listening to Marathi Lavani and devotional bhajans, preparing traditional sweets like puran poli during festivals, taking leisurely walks in the local market, attending small group discussions at the community centre, and watching classic Hindi cinema on television. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Jalna.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 64
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Jalna
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 64-year-old female from Maharashtra, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education, speaks Marathi as her first language. She resides in an urban area of Jalna district and leads a traditional lifestyle, managing household duties and community activities. Clinically, she may exhibit stress-related behaviors and dietary patterns consistent with traditional Marathi cuisine.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

निष्कर्ष: छातीचा X-ray मध्ये सौम्य कार्डिओमेगाली दिसून येते, ज्याचा कार्डिओथोरॅसिक रेशो 0.52 आहे. द्विपक्षीय पल्मोनरी व्हॅस्क्युलर कंजेशन दिसून आले आहे, ज्यामध्ये इंटरस्टिशियल मार्किंग्स सुरुवातीच्या पल्मोनरी एडेमाचे संकेत देतात. कोणतेही तीव्र न्यूमोनिक इन्फिल्ट्रेट्स किंवा प्लुरल इफ्यूजन ओळखले गेले नाहीत. पोटाच्या अल्ट्रासाऊंडमध्ये हेपॅटोमेगाली दिसून येते, ज्याचा लिव्हर स्पॅन 16 सेमी आहे, सौम्य फॅटी बदल आहेत. पित्ताशयाची भिंत जाड झाली आहे आणि त्यात अनेक लहान कॅल्कुली आहेत. मूत्रपिंड सौम्य वाढलेले दिसतात आणि त्यांची इकोजेनिसिटी टिकून आहे. कोणतेही हायड्रोनेफ्रोसिस किंवा कॅल्कुली दिसले नाहीत. थायरॉईड विखुरलेल्या स्वरूपात वाढलेला दिसतो आणि त्याची इकोटेक्चर विषम आहे. कोणतेही फोकल लीजन्स ओळखले गेले नाहीत.

payload.professional_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a seasoned community organiser and domestic specialist, leverages flawless budgeting, traditional cooking, event coordination and needlework to mentor younger women and sustain cultural rituals, while maintaining a disciplined routine and exacting standards.

payload.sports_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, who prefers gentle exercise, joins early morning walks in the local market area and attends weekly yoga sessions at the community centre, while cheering for the Mumbai Indians cricket team and occasionally playing traditional board games like Ludo with neighbours.
payload.arts_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a devoted listener of Marathi Lavani, devotional bhajans and classic Hindi films, often hums melodies of Lata Mangeshkar and Sudhir Phadke while knitting, and appreciates the theatrical performances of local Natak mandalis.
payload.travel_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, who enjoys short pilgrimages to Shirdi and Pandharpur and occasional weekend getaways to Mumbai, plans trips meticulously using word of mouth and prefers traveling by train or shared auto, balancing family commitments and limited budget.
payload.culinary_persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a seasoned home cook, excels at preparing poha, bhakri, puran poli and shrikhand, prefers using jaggery, kokum, fresh neem leaves and turmeric in her dishes, and enjoys savoring traditional sweets spiced with cardamom and garnished with marigold petals during festivals.
payload.persona: Kaushalyabai Jagannath, a meticulous 64‑year‑old matriarch who blends disciplined budgeting, traditional cooking and community leadership, yet battles chronic worries and occasional splurges on festival indulgences.
payload.cultural_background: Kaushalyabai belongs to a traditional Marathi‑speaking family settled in Jalna, Maharashtra, a region where Marathi culture intermingles with Khandeshi folk traditions. She was raised in a joint family that values hierarchical respect, community celebrations, and the observance of regional festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali, and the Khandeshi Jatra. Even though Jalna is an urban setting, her upbringing is steeped in the conventional Maharashtrian ethos of hospitality, disciplined routine, and reverence for elders.
payload.linguistic_background: As an illiterate woman, Kaushalyabai cannot read or write in any script, but she is fluent in spoken Marathi, her mother tongue, and Khandeshi, the regional dialect she uses with neighbours and relatives. She also understands basic Hindi from television and community interactions, though she relies on oral communication. Her linguistic skills are practical and centred on daily household interactions, religious rituals, and the organization of community events, where she excels in giving clear verbal instructions and remembering intricate details through mental notes.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kaushalyabai has mastered a suite of domestic and community skills through years of hands‑on experience. She excels in preparing authentic Maharashtrian dishes such as poha, bhakri, and shrikhand, maintaining strict hygienic standards in the kitchen. She manages the household budget meticulously, using mental arithmetic and simple ledger marks despite being illiterate. Her organizational talent shines in planning family events and temple festivals, where she coordinates volunteers, prepares timelines, and ensures every ritual is performed on schedule. Additionally, she is skilled in traditional needlework and embroidery, crafts herbal remedies for common ailments, and acts as a trusted mediator in neighbourhood disputes, leveraging her assertive yet compassionate demeanor.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Maharashtrian Cooking", "Household Budget Management", "Event Planning for Family and Temple", "Traditional Needlework and Embroidery", "Herbal Remedy Preparation", "Community Mediation", "Mental Arithmetic and Record-Keeping", "Gardening and Household Organization"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Kaushalyabai enjoys gardening, especially tending to marigold and neem plants, knitting simple woolen shawls, listening to Marathi Lavani and devotional bhajans, preparing traditional sweets like puran poli during festivals, taking leisurely walks in the local market, attending small group discussions at the community centre, and watching classic Hindi cinema on television. She also finds pleasure in keeping a meticulous inventory of pantry staples and organizing family photo albums.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Gardening (marigold & neem)", "Knitting woolen shawls", "Listening to Marathi Lavani and devotional bhajans", "Preparing traditional sweets (puran poli) during festivals", "Leisurely walks in the local market", "Participating in small group discussions at community centre", "Watching classic Hindi cinema on TV", "Maintaining pantry inventory and photo album organization"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Although retired, Kaushalyabai aims to continue serving her family and community by mentoring younger women in preserving traditional cooking, budgeting, and cultural rituals. She aspires to document her family's recipes and oral histories through audio recordings, ensuring they are passed down to future generations. She also seeks to strengthen the local women's self‑help group by organizing skill‑sharing workshops, thereby fostering financial independence and social support for the neighbourhood, while maintaining her own mental well‑being through a disciplined daily routine.

She resides in an urban area of Jalna district, speaks Marathi, and has a history of traditional domestic management. Clinically, she presents with chronic anxiety and occasional dietary indiscretions during festivals.

Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.

- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.

- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]

- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]

[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Bai Patil]] [[AGE|64]] [[GENDER|Female]] वॉर्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] बेड [[BED_NUMBER|04]]

व्हायटल्स / तीव्रता: रुग्ण शुद्धीत, सतर्क, जागरूक आहे आणि मध्यम त्रासात आहे. बीपी 140/90 mmHg, एचआर 108/min, आरआर 22/min, SpO2 96% रूम एअरवर, तापमान 101.2°F. उजव्या खालच्या भागात वेदना स्कोअर 7/10 आहे. पोटात दुखणे आणि स्नायूंचा ताण जाणवत आहे.

नर्सिंग नोट्स:
रुग्णाला पती रमेश पाटील यांनी अँब्युलन्सने आणले. रुग्ण सांगतो की सकाळीपासून तीव्र पोटाचे दुखणे सुरू झाले आहे, सोबत मळमळ आणि उलट्यांचा एक प्रसंग झाला आहे. वेदना तीव्र, सतत आहेत आणि पाठीकडे पसरत आहेत. ताप किंवा थंडी वाजल्याची तक्रार नाही. रुग्ण कोणतीही अलीकडील शस्त्रक्रिया किंवा दुखापत नसल्याचे सांगतो. शेवटची शौचास क्रिया काल झाली होती. रुग्णाला उच्च रक्तदाब आणि अधूनमधून बद्धकोष्ठतेचा इतिहास आहे. तपासणी केल्यावर, पोट कडक आहे आणि उजव्या खालच्या भागात रिव्हर्स टेंडरनेस जाणवत आहे. इतरत्र पेरिटोनाइटिसची कोणतीही लक्षणे आढळली नाहीत. रुग्ण अस्वस्थ आहे आणि वेदना कमी करण्याची विनंती करत आहे. डाव्या हाताच्या पुढच्या भागात 18G कॅन्युलासह आयव्ही ॲक्सेस स्थापित केला. रुग्णाला आयव्ही फ्लुइड्स सुरू केले - 0.9% नॉर्मल सलाईन 100 ml/hr वेगाने. प्रोटोकॉलनुसार वेदनाशामक औषधे दिली - मॉर्फिन 4 mg आयव्ही. रुग्णाला एनपीओ राहण्याचा सल्ला दिला. रक्त नमुने CBC, LFT आणि सीरम इलेक्ट्रोलाइट्ससाठी पाठवले. सर्जिकल कन्सल्ट मिळवला. रुग्णाच्या व्हायटल्सवर दर 15 मिनिटांनी लक्ष ठेवले जात आहे. नातेवाईकाला रुग्णाच्या स्थितीबद्दल आणि पुढील तपासणीच्या योजनेबद्दल माहिती दिली. रुग्णाचा [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] सिस्टममध्ये अपडेट केला आहे.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Use ONLY allow-list TYPE names. Billing aliases: EMAIL/CONTACT_EMAIL → EMAIL_ADDRESS; ADDRESS_NUMBER → RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS; RECEIPT_NUMBER and STATE are NOT types (receipt no. / state stay plain prose; geography uses DISTRICT).

- PAN must match ^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{4}[A-Z]$ e.g. ABCDE1234F — not KAAPR1234C-style junk.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Maternal_health must be age-plausible: routine ANC/delivery for ages ~18–40; ages 40–42 → high-risk ANC / PNC / contraception — never invent extreme late pregnancy (e.g. 49yo as routine 28-week gestation).

- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content;
  geriatric / extreme-age routine 28-week pregnancy.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; maternal content is
  age-plausible (no unsafe extreme late pregnancy as a routine story).

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs (billing: EMAIL→EMAIL_ADDRESS, ADDRESS→RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS;
  receipt/state/money stay plain prose). Valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=West Bengal, district=Darjiling.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 18
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Labourer, Agriculture
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Darjiling
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: An 18-year-old female labourer in agriculture from a rural West Bengal district, never married, with primary education. She speaks Nepali as her first language and has limited Bengali proficiency. Clinically, she presents with occupational exposure to agricultural environments and potential nutritional deficiencies from a diet reliant on staple grains.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
नुस्खा — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|North Bengal Cancer Centre]]
बिरामी [[PATIENT_NAME|Sunita Thapa]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|NBCC-2024-0156]]
डा. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
फोन: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9832145678]]

[[DISTRICT|Darjiling]] को बिरामीमा नयाँ निदान गरिएको एक्युट लिम्फोब्लास्टिक ल्युकेमिया भएको छ। प्रारम्भिक जाँचमा ब्लास्ट सहित WBC 85,000, हेमोग्लोबिन 7.2 g/dL, प्लेटलेट्स 25,000 देखिएको छ।

नुस्खा:
- Vincristine 2 mg IV साप्ताहिक
- Prednisone 60 mg PO दैनिक 28 दिनका लागि
- L-Asparaginase 2000 IU/m² IM दिन 1, 3, 5, 15, 17, 19 मा
- Methotrexate 3000 mg/m² IV दिन 15 मा
- Methotrexate पछि 24 घण्टाबाट सुरु हुने Leucovorin उद्धार

सहयोगी उपचार:
- Filgrastim 5 mcg/kg SC दैनिक दिन 5 बाट सुरु
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO TID आवश्यकता अनुसार वाकवाकी
- Pantoprazole 40 mg PO दैनिक
- Hb <7 g/dL भएमा आवश्यकता अनुसार रगत चढाउने

फलो-अप: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0315-001]] 15 मार्च 2024 मा केमोथेरापी चक्र 2 का लागि।

[[PATIENT_ID|PID-NBCC-2024-0089]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Tea Garden Area, Kurseong, West Bengal 734201]]
payload.generated_text_en:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|North Bengal Cancer Centre]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sunita Thapa]], [[AGE|18]] / [[GENDER|Female]], MRN [[MRN|NBCC-2024-0156]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9832145678]]

Patient from [[DISTRICT|Darjiling]] presents with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Initial workup shows WBC 85,000 with blasts, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, platelets 25,000.

Rx:
- Vincristine 2 mg IV weekly
- Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 28 days
- L-Asparaginase 2000 IU/m² IM on days 1, 3, 5, 15, 17, 19
- Methotrexate 3000 mg/m² IV on day 15
- Leucovorin rescue starting 24 hours after methotrexate

Supportive care:
- Filgrastim 5 mcg/kg SC daily starting day 5
- Ondansetron 8 mg PO TID PRN nausea
- Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
- Blood transfusion as needed for Hb <7 g/dL

Follow-up: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0315-001]] on 15th March 2024 for chemotherapy cycle 2.

[[PATIENT_ID|PID-NBCC-2024-0089]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
[[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Tea Garden Area, Kurseong, West Bengal 734201]]

payload.professional_persona: Geeta Das, a seasoned forest practitioner in Jalpaiguri, they harness meticulous oral record‑keeping and a deep curiosity for native flora, using basic surveying tools and community coordination to drive sustainable forestry while competitively mentoring younger women for senior forester roles.
payload.linguistic_persona: Geeta Das communicates fluently in Nepali, they understand Hindi and Bengali well enough for everyday conversations, and despite being illiterate, they rely on oral tradition to convey instructions and maintain forest logs.
payload.sports_persona: Geeta Das enjoys early‑morning walks through tea‑garden trails and follows football passionately, cheering for Mohun Bagan and supporting the local Jalpaiguri cricket club, while they occasionally join a community yoga group for flexibility.
payload.arts_persona: Geeta Das immerses themselves in local folk music, often humming the melodies of Nepali legends Narayan Gopal and Raju Pariyar, while they craft clay figurines inspired by traditional puppetry and paint vivid scenes of forest life reminiscent of Amrita Sher‑Gill’s brushwork.
payload.travel_persona: Geeta Das prefers modest, self‑planned treks to nearby hill stations like Darjeeling and the tea‑garden trails of Kalimpong, while they dream of a cross‑border Himalayan pilgrimage to Nepal’s Annapurna region, and they balance their love for nature with the need to keep travel costs low for their family.
payload.culinary_persona: Geeta Das enjoys hearty dal‑bhat with gundruk leaves, spicy Nepali momo filled with minced chicken and garlic, and tea flavored with cardamom and ginger, though they prefer buying ready‑made snacks from the local market rather than cooking elaborate meals.
payload.persona: Geeta Das is a methodical forest guardian whose curiosity for folk art fuels competitive mentorship, who balances cash‑only spending with modest gold savings, and who, despite being illiterate, they navigate daily life through oral tradition while occasionally forgetting follow‑up health checks.
payload.cultural_background: Geeta belongs to the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community in the tea‑garden belt of Jalpaiguri, West Bengal. Her cultural upbringing blends traditional Hindu customs from the hills with the rural Bengali milieu, celebrating festivals such as Dashain, Tihar, and Durga Puja, and preserving local folk songs and crafts.
payload.linguistic_background: Geeta is illiterate but speaks Nepali fluently, which is her mother tongue. She can communicate effectively in Nepali for daily tasks and forest work. Through community interaction she has functional comprehension of Hindi and Bengali, allowing her to follow simple instructions and engage in informal conversations, though she cannot read or write in any script.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Geeta has practical expertise in forest management, including tree planting, sapling care, trail maintenance, fire prevention, and community coordination for forest conservation. She is adept at using basic surveying tools, identifying native tree species, and applying traditional knowledge of medicinal plants. Her methodical approach ensures systematic record‑keeping through oral logs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['forest surveying', 'tree planting', 'sapling care', 'trail maintenance', 'fire prevention', 'community outreach', 'identifying native species', 'traditional medicinal plant knowledge', 'basic GPS use', 'record-keeping']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Geeta has a deep interest in art, especially local folk music and handicrafts. She enjoys trekking in the forest, identifying flowers and herbs, gardening, and listening to traditional Nepali songs. In her free time she makes small clay figures, paints, and participates in village festivals.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['folk music listening', 'handicraft making', 'trekking in forests', 'gardening', 'plant identification', 'traditional Nepali cooking', 'painting', 'community festivals', 'storytelling']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Geeta aspires to become a senior forester and lead community‑driven conservation projects in the Jalpaiguri forest region. She aims to acquire informal training in sustainable forestry, improve fire management practices, and mentor younger women from her community. She also wishes to document local folk art tied to forest culture, hoping to blend her curiosity for art with her professional role.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Geeta Das is a 48-year-old female forest worker in rural West Bengal, currently married, with an illiterate education level. She lives in Jalpaiguri district and speaks Nepali as her first language, with functional Hindi and Bengali comprehension. Geeta’s lifestyle includes cash-only spending, modest gold savings, and a preference for self-planned local treks.
